So let me, um, let me, you know, let me kind of share with you some things while we're, while we're going into, let me see the next person who's coming up next. So the next person is, um, is Luke at 730. So first and foremost, um, we're going to be able to take a break for 15 minutes in a little bit, um, before Luke comes on for sure. So maybe at about seven o'clock, I'll, I'll, we'll take a 15 minute break and then come back and then get ready for Luke who's going to talk about using direct mail to, um, really pump up your sales even, um, even more. And, um, but for right now, let me, let me share a couple things. So, um, the first thing that I want to talk about is affiliate marketing. And I know that this is going to piss a lot of people off, but I'm okay with that because the reality is, um, that I want everybody to hear it because I believe everybody needs to hear it. And so the first thing that we're going to cover is, uh, affiliate marketing. And then depending on our time, I'll go into some of the other things that I don't typically talk about publicly just because I don't want to step on anybody's toes. So one of also the common questions, um, one of the most common questions that I've been asked on top of, um, you know, how to create products and turn my knowledge into, uh, you know, an info product or a coaching program is this idea of, well, you know, can I use your system for affiliate marketing? You know, I'm an affiliate marketer and I want to use your, uh, your six-figure formula for affiliate marketing. And so I always, the answer that I give is it depends. You know, it depends if, if all they're doing is brokering traffic, meaning all they're doing is placing an ad on Facebook, let's just say, and they're direct linking to a CPA offer, to an offer on ClickBank, to someone's offer, um, then I always say no, you can't because all you're doing is brokering traffic. The reality is that, um, that affiliate marketing, the way the majority of marketers do it, affiliate marketing is not a business. It's an income stream. It's an income opportunity. There's lots of different ways to generate income online. I'm not about teaching people how to make money online. I'm about teaching people how to build a money-making business online. And there's a big difference, right? There's lots of different ways, hacks, um, things that you can take advantage of every now and then that'll allow you to make money, right? Like there's a lot of people that are jumping on, you know, Teespring on, um, on Facebook and they're making money, um, you know, with Teespring offers and that's cool. There's nothing wrong with having a side income stream that you play around with if it interests you, but it should never be your primary source of income. It should never be your primary business. You know, pure affiliate marketers that are driving traffic to somebody else's offer, that's not a business. That's not a build, a business. There's no long-term equity being built if you're not generating a list of customers for yourself. The equity, the long-term equity, the long-term value in an online business is in the customer relationship, right? The, the, you heard, I think it was Jason say, and you've heard me say this, my gosh, you know, especially those of you that have been around for any length of time, the most expensive activity in any business is the acquisition of new customers. Acquiring new customers is the most expensive activity, right? And so there's a reason why companies will willingly pay you to be their affiliate and to generate traffic so that they can generate a sale. There's a reason why. It's not because they want to do a good thing for you. It's not because they want to see you make money. No, it's because they know that the value is in the customer, the real value, the real money is in having the customer and being able to sell to that customer over and over and over. It's not, the real value is not in the initial transaction, right? It's not in initially acquiring the customer. Why? Because all of your acquisition costs, all of your acquisition expenses are in the front end. All of the acquisition costs are felt, are experienced when you acquire a customer, right? And so these companies will happily allow you to drive the traffic, do the legwork, take all of the risk, and they'll happily pay you 50%, 80%, upwards of 100% for you to do all the legwork to get them a new customer. Why? Because they know that everything else that they sell those people, especially if they're selling information, is pure profit. That's the money. The money, the profit in an information business especially, is on the back end. The profit is in really, in any business, is on the back end because all of the customer acquisition costs were absorbed on the front, right? So if you've heard me say before that, you know, look, and you heard Jason actually say the same thing, he said, if I can break even on the front, if I can break even when I acquire new customers, I'm cool with that. I'm fine with that. If I can spend $500 to generate customers and I only make back $500, that's cool. I'm okay with that. He's okay with that because he just acquired customers for free. He acquired new customers for free. And the asset is the customer, right? You've heard people say, let me say it like this, you've heard people say before that the list online is your most valuable asset, right? You've heard people say, your email list is your most valuable asset. That's wrong. That's the old school internet marketer list builder mindset. Your greatest, most valuable asset is your customer list. That's where the money is. The money in your business is in the customer list. The profit in your business is in selling those customers more and more products. So you can deliver more and more value. So you can help them more and more. That's where the money is. And so affiliate marketers that are only doing affiliate marketing and not building up their customer list, they're at a tremendous disadvantage. Number one, pure affiliates, affiliates that are generating traffic and sending direct to an offer. Number one, okay, unless they are getting 100% of the money, like unless they're being paid 100% instead of 50% or 60%, they have less money that they can afford to invest to generate a customer. Like if I'm the product owner and I am promoting the product and somebody is an affiliate and they're a pure affiliate and they're getting 50%, they can only afford to spend up to 50% to generate a new customer. I can afford to spend double what they do to generate the same exact customer. Why? Because it's my product. Why? Because it's all my money. And so right away, the affiliate is at a gross disadvantage because they can only afford to spend up to whatever percentage of commission they are getting. Second, they are at a disadvantage because if they're not controlling what happens after that initial product is purchased, well then they don't get to make additional money through the transaction maximization sequence or what some marketers use as an upsell sequence. They don't have the opportunity to do that and so that's less money that they can extract. Pay close attention here as well for the affiliate to make money profit. If they're getting, let's say, $50 a sale, that's their commission. Whether that $50 is 50%, 60%, or 100%, it's insignificant. Let's say they're getting $50 per sale. Well, that means that they can't afford to spend up the $50 to make the sale happen because if they do, they don't generate any profit. Whereas the business owner knows that all the profit is on the back end. All the profit is on the back end. You see, the value of generating a new customer is that every time now that you've got that customer, a customer is a customer, a customer is a buyer. As long as you deliver stellar value, exceed their expectations, deliver the goods, fulfill on your promises, those people are going to buy more and more and more from you if you're delivering exceptional value. Right? And so the real money is made in the second transaction with a customer, the third transaction, the fourth transaction, the fifth transaction, and so on. It's not in the first transaction, but yet the affiliate only has the first transaction to make that money from. And so they're at a gross disadvantage. It's why I tell people that, like, look, the money isn't having your own product, whether it's an info product or a physical product. The money is not in being an affiliate for somebody else. When you are an affiliate for somebody else and you think that that's your business, what you're really doing is building their business. That you're building their business because you're building their customer base, you're building their back end for them, you're building somebody else's business, not your own business. Right? And that was really just as a quick aside. That was one of the things that drove me to become an entrepreneur. Number one, there were two things that drove me to become an entrepreneur many, many moons ago when I was still living in New Jersey. And I worked for a great company. I had a great job, still have a great relationship with the owners of the company. They actually ironically brought me in and had me do paid consulting for them after I left as an employee. But two things made me leave my job. Number one was the desire for autonomy. Right? I wanted freedom. I wanted to wake up when I wanted, work when I wanted, go to sleep when I wanted, take lunch when I wanted. I wanted to be able to go, watch my kids play volleyball, dance recitals, you know, all that stuff. I wanted to be able to go meet my wife in the afternoon for lunch, have breakfast with them, wear what I wanted, you know, take days off, go on vacation when I wanted the whole night. That was the first thing. The second thing was I didn't want to make anybody else rich. I was tired of making other people a lot of money. And so I said, I want to do this for myself. To me, affiliate marketing is the same thing. Affiliate marketing is making other people money because you are growing their business because you're growing their customer base. That's why they're doing it. That's why they offer an affiliate program. They offer an affiliate program because they'll happily give you money to do all the light work to go out and build their customer base that they're now going to make a tremendous amount of profit on in the back end because every savvy direct response marketer knows the real business begins after the first transaction. Right? The real business that we're in is the back end. The most difficult part of every business, the most difficult part of every business is the acquisition of a customer. Right? It's why my friend Mark Ford, Michael Masterson, he would say that until you've got your marketing funnel in place, until you've got your conversion vehicle in place, until you can turn a prospect into a customer, nothing else is important. Nothing else is the priority. Don't worry about an office. Don't worry about a phone. Don't worry about a fax machine. Don't worry about setting up branding. Don't worry about a logo. Don't worry about getting five new computers. Don't worry about a virtual assistant. Don't worry about outsourcing. Don't worry about any of those things until you've got a mechanism in place that can convert prospects into customers for you. That's the only thing that matters. That is the foundation of your business. It's also the hardest and most expensive aspect of your business. That's the single greatest area that causes most marketers to struggle and not really make big money or any money online. They get the technical stuff. Maybe they even have access to tools, but they don't have a marketing funnel in place that converts cold traffic. That's why I'll also tell you, that's why, let me say something. I told you, it's obvious I love product launches. I just did one and we're finishing it up. It closes tonight, which side note, if you haven't gotten into the Six Figure Funnel Formula Training Program yet, you should go ahead and do that. Click the green button and jump in. I love product launches. Like I said, I was an instructor for a faculty member for Jeff Walker at his $10,000 per head product launch manager workshop years ago. I was a case study for him very early on where I launched a Six Figure business with a list of like 350 people in the massage therapy niche. Here's the thing, that if your business is reliant on product launches or if your business is reliant on affiliates, you don't have a business. You have a promotion. That's one of the pieces of advice that Rich Sheffrin gave me when I sat down with them many, many moons ago over a cup of coffee in a Starbucks cafe in a Barnes & Noble here in Boca Raton, Florida. Like product launches are great, but they're the icing. They're the seasoning. They're the extra. The reality is that until you can acquire new customers from paid traffic, you don't have a business. You have a promotion. If you have to rely on affiliates, you don't have a business. You have a promotion that can bring you money when affiliates promote. But what about when they flake out? I can tell you this. I'll tell you for this launch, we had over 400 affiliates that signed up to promote. Do you know how many promoted? Less than 50. So 400 signed up. I share that with you because I want you to understand that those businesses, those things like product launches are reliance on those. A reliance on product launches. A reliance on affiliates. Those things make your business extremely vulnerable because they are at the whim of third party people. Excuse me. Everything is dependent on what your affiliates do. What your partners do. Do they promote? Do they promote the way they're supposed to? Do they promote with the right swipe files, with the right banners, with the right ads? Do they promote at the right time? Do they promote as fully as they say they're going to? Do they promote at all? Do they flake out and go somewhere else and get somebody else's offer? Do they write like and I had a good friend, a good friend of mine who happens to be the godfather of my youngest daughter. He was doing over a million dollars a year and the way he was doing it was he had an affiliate manager that set him up with webinars, with partners and he would just do webinar after webinar after webinar after webinar each week. They would do webinars. They'd sell 40, 50, $60,000 worth of his software, his web-based software application and yeah, they grew the thing to over a million dollars and I said to him, listen to me. I said, that's great and I'm so happy to see you that you crossed over into the seven figures. I said, but right now your business is extremely vulnerable and you do not have a real business because you don't have a customer acquisition vehicle that is not reliant on other people. You have a promotion that you're now leveraging. You have a cool promotion, a cool webinar. That's what you have that when partners are willing to let you share that webinar with their people, you make money. But what happens if they decide not to? Your business will evaporate. Your business will shrink overnight and he didn't listen to me. He was enamored with the success that they were having. Fast forward and exactly what I said would happen where less and less partners began to promote less and less and less and less and less and less and he eventually got to the point where he had to sell the business for close to what he used to do on a single webinar and he ended up having to sell this because he did not put the right systems in place. He did not have a conversion vehicle, a marketing funnel that allowed him to generate cold traffic, that allowed him to invest a dollar into paid media and turn that dollar into sales and customers. And so you need to have that. That's why there is nothing more important than having your funnel. Once you have your funnel and you see that you can invest a dollar into traffic and generate at least a dollar back in the form of a new customer and sales, the whole game will forever change for you. Because then it's about, well, how can I quickly dump more and more people into this funnel? How can I scale up my traffic? How can I use Facebook more? How can I use the things that Charles Kirkland can teach me? Jonathan Misell can teach me. How can I tap into these other traffic generation sources? And so the entire game changes. That's the key to growth and that's why companies like Agora, they very rarely, if ever, do product launches. They very rarely, they don't work with affiliates and partners like that. Why? Because they don't need to because they're able to generate cold traffic and turn that traffic into money. It's just like, look, it's just like any real business, a brick and mortar business. A brick and mortar business, if you had a friend or a family member that was going to open up a business, a pizzeria or whatever, and when you said to them, well, what's your plan? What's your marketing plan to get new customers? And they said, well, I'm going to rely on my friends telling their friends. That's what we're going to do, which is the equivalent of, I'm going to rely on partners introducing me to their list. What would you say to that family member? You would say, either one, don't invest your money in that business if that's your plan, or two, you've got to come up with a real plan because that's not a business. You relying on your friends to send you referrals because what happens if your friends don't send you referrals? What happens if they flake out? What happens if they move? What happens if they get out of the referral business? And so you've got to recognize that the same thing goes for your business. Inconsistencies and income, inconsistencies in cash flow, all of those things are directly linked to your not having a marketing funnel. You want a reliable business, you want reliable income, you want steady cash flow, you want to know that you can extract X number of dollars from your business every single week, every single month, right? Well, you have to have a sales funnel, excuse me, a marketing funnel in place. You have to have a marketing funnel in place. Now I'll tell you that a lot of marketers when you hear, excuse me, when you hear direct mail, you might either immediately tune out thinking this is dated, it's old school, it doesn't apply to me, or you might think I have no room for direct mail in my business, or it's expensive, or it's a lot of work. Well, remember keep in mind what I said earlier that anybody can discount an idea. It takes the real savvy entrepreneur to really see how strategies and tactics and methods and systems can be applied to your business. I'll tell you that for most internet marketers, there is a tremendous opportunity with direct mail. That's why I went to Luke. And that's why I had on Skype Luke and Andy to talk to Luke about direct mail because there is a tremendous opportunity, right? The, you know, there is a huge amount of clutter and noise in the inbox, in the inbox today, the email inbox. There's a tremendous amount of clutter and noise. There are spam filters, right? There is a tremendous amount of competition for attention. Yet there's nowhere near that same level of attention, competition for attention in the mailbox. And so it gives you a tremendous opportunity to leverage that and turn it into greater and bigger sales and go deeper with your back end. And so we're really in for a treat with Luke. Now the other thing to think about as it relates to direct mail, excuse me, is that not all more, and this is a huge, this is kind of a heavy lesson, but you know, but not all marketing channels are created equally in terms of the credibility that they are assigned. What I mean by that is that not all marketing channels are viewed with the same level of credibility that other channels are. For example, really the truth of the matter is that especially for the older demographic, but just really in general across the board, the least credible channel is the internet, right? The internet is one of the least credible channels of all of the channels, right? Like for example, print media is viewed with a lot more credibility than the internet is, right? So if people read something in a magazine, in a newspaper, in a trade journal, there's a lot more credibility that they naturally assign to that message compared to if they read the same thing online, right? Because people know that anybody can throw up a website, that it's pretty much still even the Wild West today, and that anybody can virtually say anything at any point. You never know what to believe, right? Whereas you very rarely see that in print, right? There are also different regulations, different laws that apply to print compared to the internet. And so when it comes to credibility, the internet is really lowest on the list. What is higher on the list than the internet is direct mail, right? There are also laws and regulations that govern what you can and can't say in direct mail. Laws and regulations that do not apply to online marketers. The online marketers can do and say virtually whatever they want, and depending on where they're located in other countries and whatnot, there's little that we can do to punish them. The same rules, the same does not apply to direct mail. So reading something via a direct mail letter, it not only is more apt to grab the attention of your prospect or of your customer because there's less competition, it's also viewed with a lot more credibility. And so there's tremendous value to each and every one of you learning more about how to best leverage direct mail, and it's not the kind of thing that you would use initially with, excuse me, your new six-figure funnel. They're starting, let's say, over the next weeks to make some money and that money is growing and they're experiencing more and more success. And now they want to take things up a notch and really, really grow there the results that they're getting. And so how do they begin thinking about incorporating direct mail? Okay, so obviously that's a very broad question. So I'll give you as much detail as they can and sort of tailor it depending on what it is that they're selling. So if you're selling something digital, one of the ways that you can add a whole lot of credibility and reduce refunds and reduce charge backs and reduce, you know, people having an unpleasant experience with your product is by doing follow-up with direct mail. So as a starting point, if you're starting online, at the very least, you should be, I always tell people that I'm working with, you should always be in your process, you should be collecting their physical address. So let's say that you're marketing something and you're using one of the platforms that doesn't do that, like ClickBank or something like that. You've got to engineer into your funnel a way to still get that address. Several ClickBank people and people with big offers that are doing huge numbers have been doing that for years and it's smart. You need to be able to not only gather their email address and their phone and everything else that you can get, but the address, the physical address is one of those things that you can use for a lot of things. So the number one way to think about it in your funnel is to stand out from all of your competitors. So if there's competitors in your marketplace, I guarantee you they're not direct mailing their customers. And certainly not as a thank you. I'm talking about starting off with something that's nothing more than a thank you card if you're selling something digital. If you're selling something physical where you're actually selling it online but you're delivering it physically to them, that every single mail piece that goes out should have a mail piece in it. It doesn't matter whether that mail piece is designed to communicate dynamic customer service and the fact that you really care about them, or whether it's literally as a bounce back letter you've got something in there that's the first upsell. I was going to say, if I'm thinking about a brand new business owner that's starting to market online, that's the first sort of thing I would be thinking about is no matter what I'm doing to generate that sale, I want to make sure that I'm collecting an address because there's going to be lots of different things you can do with it. So okay, so for the folks that let's say are selling on ClickBank or JD Zoo or something like that, when you talk about they have to engineer something into their funnel so that they're able to collect the address. So for example what some marketers do when they use ClickBank or JD Zoo is the thank you page that people come to after they pay via ClickBank or whatever in order to get access to the product they got to complete one more step and that's maybe where they're collecting address from. And that's one way to do it, the other way to do it is instead of making it something that is where you're actually putting a barrier in front of them, you can make it that you're giving them something wonderful for giving you the address. So the one that I saw do it for a long time and they're no longer selling on ClickBank but they had a good six or seven year run, what they did was after the sales process and I believe it was either on the thank you page or it might have been one click after the thing. So the way that they had a set up is we've got a really special gift we want to get to you but we've got to send it to you and we can't give it to you through the computer. And so they had practically 100% opt-in rate and give them the address. So what they were doing is they had really big ticket upsells afterwards and they had fantastic follow up but they wanted to be able to collect phone number and address so that they could make contact every way possible because they knew that their upsells were really where their money was. The front end was profitable but it wasn't where the real money was, the real money was in the follow up via phone, direct mail and also email. Okay so now let's talk about for one second keeping things really basic because the majority of folks on here right now are just getting their first nice profitable strategic six figure funnel up and running. So let's say they're collecting the full address, the full contact details and so they start with a thank you postcard. Now let's just talk about the thank you postcard for a second because we'll talk a little bit about fulfillment on that, like how do you actually do that. So is there any kind of, is it a pure, let me just ask it, let me just say it like it is, is it a pure thank you postcard with no other strategy behind it other than just selling it? It depends on what else, what your next step in your funnel is going to look like. For some people yes it is, it is just a pure thank you. For others it's a thank you and an ask for something and so the ask for something can be you know literally another sale and so a lot of times when I, we sold several thousand, tens of thousands of units of one particular product and what we used in there is the bounce back, we call it a bounce back. So a bounce back is literally a sales letter that goes out in the package because it's bouncing it back to I don't know where the term came from, some old direct response guy. But literally what you would do is the very first offer would be right there and so that's one way to do it. You can also do the pure thank you. If you're going to set up bigger ticket sales later, most of the time you want to set that up with a pure thank you. So if you've got something to sell later that's in the $1,000 plus range, $800 plus range, it's best to start with just a pure thank you. Because you don't want every single communication coming from you to be one where they feel like you know your hands out looking for another buck. There are marketers that will disagree with me about that but I feel strongly and based on experience that if you have lower ticket sales it's no problem to ask for an order on pretty much every communication. But if you're looking for bigger ticket sales you've got to have some give, give, give in between as what Gary Vaynerchuk called it, jab, jab, jab before you write hook. Yeah, very cool. I dig that. Okay, so now let's talk about, so let's say let's go with the pure thank you, right? Like you're going to do something that most internet marketers don't do. You're going to thank them offline in a way that gets attention, that stands out, that differentiates you. And so how does a marketer, a newer marketer that doesn't have access to something like an infusion soft or an office auto pilot, how does that get fulfilled? Are there, like what, go ahead. Well in the old, I mean I've been doing this a long time, I mean for a long time. So depending on the volume, if the volume is small enough you can still do that and stuff in house. I mean you can do mail merges in Word, it's not a big deal. Sure. Okay. Okay, so, you know, this again, that's an open-ended question because it depends on, you know, if I was looking at like the price points of each product that you've had, I think it would be easier to fit them in. But just generally speaking, generally speaking, I don't want you to think that direct mail is one of those things that you need to use for every single communication because it is certainly a more expensive way to communicate with people than sending them an email. But here's the beauty of it, and I think you touched on this earlier before I came on about, you know, the email, the inbox being cluttered and the mailbox being empty. You can communicate with people through the mail, especially once they're a customer. Now cold mailing, that's a whole other subject, you can get into that here in a minute. But communicating with your customer base, if you've done a good job from the beginning, it will be a 100% open rate. They're not going to chuck it in the mail. They're not going to say, oh, Todd sent me a letter. I don't think I'm going to open that. It's just not going to happen unless they hate you. And if they hate you, it's because you did something wrong before that step, right? So as long as you've done all those steps right, the direct mail is the best way to get them, get their attention and get a 100% open rate from customers, obviously not from, you know, cold mail. So the next place that I would use that, let's assume that you did a thank you, and then you've had, you know, maybe you've got your email sequence here that you've got a series of one or two educational emails, and then you're going for the next thing that you're going to sell. Probably the next spot where I would use direct mail with them is if you've got something that is $600, $700 somewhere in that range or more. And what I would do, and I've seen this done a lot, is actually put together, and there's companies that will do this for you. You don't have to do this in-house or you can do it in-house. But you can actually put together little invitations, invitations to events. Invitations to events that get to somebody like that are going to have a show-up rate that is far above anything you've ever seen with email, because it's treated as special and different. And if the event is actually a little special and different, you know, that's just an added bonus. Close rate's going to be higher. Everything's going to be higher because you've done more than what the regular person does. Why did he send this to me? Well, this must be special. You know, it's a special invitation. We've done them like in black envelopes and they go out so that, you know, you can't miss it. And it says special invitation on it. And it's like, whoa, am I getting invited to some kind of, you know, black tie affair? And you can literally, you know, treat it like that. Yeah. Yeah, it's so awesome to hear you say that because it's kind of funny that in, we have a training that I released a handful of weeks ago called the Intensification Process. And it's basically a process. It's the marketing that takes place after somebody registers for a webinar in between registration and show. And this whole process, which was first really fleshed out while I was, you know, running the marketing at strategic profits, this whole process is designed to get people to show up, right, to jack up the attendance rate. And really at the foundation of that whole intensification process is exactly what you're saying is it's all about showing up differently. It's all about, right? It's not just what is the message that you're communicating, but is it's also how is that message perceived, right? Like Luke just said, everybody, you know, when somebody gets a card, you know, it's black envelope and it looks official and it's a real deal invitation. Their whole perspective, their whole perception of what this is all about is, oh, this is different. This isn't the ordinary. This isn't more of the same. This isn't another email that I got from somebody. This is something, obviously, I should pay attention to. This is something I need to look at. This is something I need to take part in. And so it's stuff like that that separates, again, the extraordinary from the average. The average, like we talked about much earlier on, the average marketer is going to shoot out an email because it's easy, because it's quick, because there's no money involved. But it's the pro. It's the A player that's going to understand, yeah, it might take more work to create this invitation. Yes, there might be some outlay of initial money that has to happen in order for this invitation to go out. But the end result is you'll make more money back. You'll develop a deeper and better relationship with your customers. You'll have a better show rate if that invitation is for an event. And you'll make much more money in the long run. That sounds really trivial, but if somebody just spent $3,000, $5,000, $10,000, the little bit of reciprocity that comes from that tin of cookies, or it could be like what Frank does, where it's a couple of books that come from Amazon or whatever, that goes so far. If you just want to get technical with the stats, it reduces refund rates in half every time, and it's just such a small thing to do. Okay, cool man. Yeah, and so what I would do with that is if the mail is going out, and this is the way it should be, if the mail is going out to people who were sent the mail piece, now what I would do with the email follow up and the online letter is I would literally mail them an email saying, hey, send you a letter, describe it, hey, it was in a yellow envelope, red, right, you couldn't miss it, it came from me. I wouldn't then dress up the online sales letter so that it looked like much different than the one that they got in the mail. Okay, okay, so you want it to look as close to your similar look and feel. Okay, and so now at what point, so you send out this initial direct mail piece, you know, sales letter to your customers, then maybe you follow that up with an email that sends them to an online version of that same sales letter. Do you then, are you kind of going back and forth between online and offline, like would you then send them a postcard or? Probably not, probably it's going to be one mail piece and the rest is going to be digital follow up and really that's just a function of the money. If they weren't moved by the first mail piece, they're probably not going to be moved by the second any more than they would if you sent them an email and took them to a digital letter. Okay. But again, none of this part is hard and fast. Sure. If we were talking about specific campaigns and we had, you know, and I knew what they looked like and all that kind of stuff, then obviously it would be a little bit different. So for example, I was just thinking of something right when you're talking and now it's just going to give me one second. You talked about the mail piece going out and then balancing back. So one thing that we didn't talk about that is fantastic in front end cold mailings, but it's even more effective sometimes with an in-house mailing to your house file is doing mailings where you've got some sort of grabber or something that is different about it than just being a plain letter. Part of that, you know, a mail piece and this really matters in the cold mail because you want to get it open, but with the house file it matters because you're making an impression. So just like with email, you've got to get it open, right? The subject line is what gets it open. If in direct mail, what gets it open is the way that you've laid out that envelope, right? The color of envelope, the type of envelope, whether you use the label or not, did you use the return address, what the postage looked like. All of that stuff matters in getting it open just like all of the little fine details do when you get an email open in the subject line. Then once you get it open, so because it's a house file, it's getting pretty damn easy to get it open. You'll get nearly 100% open. You don't have to worry about that so much, but if they open it and it looks immediately like anything else that they've thought of that they can immediately dismiss, you're probably not going to get the full thing read. So a grabber is really good to get them into the copy. A grabber can be anything from a photo, if you've got something that works, you can use the old Gary Halbert dollar bill letters. We've used those a lot. They work fantastic. For a house file, you don't have to worry about like, oh, am I wasting a bunch of money because they really make them convert. If you go through any of the old Gary Halbert newsletters, he has just millions and zillion ideas for grabbers, little bag of sand when they were selling real estate. That aspirin foreign currency, there's a ton. Just for everybody, what Luke is talking about is really when somebody opens the letter, the first thing that they see is a dollar bill attached to it or a weird piece of currency or a weird picture or an aspirin or a bag of sand or a bandaid or a rubber band. It's a grabber because it grabs their attention. The key with that, the key is of course you guys and gals. They start to tie into your book. Yeah, right. You can't just have a rubber band attached to the top and just not acknowledge why. You get to the end at the PS that says, oh, about the rubber band, I just needed you to read the letter. PS, I might have dropped my rubber band in here. If you found it, please let me know. While we're talking about this, there's so much that, because I love direct mail. I'm fascinated by it. I've kept every piece of solid direct response, direct mail that I've received in the last seven years. It's bordering on hoarding at this point. I had nearly a warehouse full for a while. I finally got rid of this stuff. I'm like, yeah, it's crazy. I've got it in my office. I've got it in my garage. It's at a point where I really should get rid of it. What I love about direct mail and what I think warrants the study of direct mail is that there's an investment involved. The people that are doing direct mail, especially when you see a direct mail piece that becomes a control or you just see it more than once, you know that it's working because they're investing money. Whereas online, people are shooting out emails, people are throwing up web pages. There's very little risk. There's very little investment. You don't really know what's working. But with direct mail, you see, especially with certain companies, whether it's Boardroom, whether it's Agora, you see the pros and you see what's working because they're investing money to send it to you over and over. It really is extremely fascinating. Before the internet, the way that we would get inside information about what other people are mailing is, if you use the direct response printer, it's hanging out at your print shop and you see what's being printed. You see what's being printed week after week. I got lots of swipe files from my printer that I used for seven years. That is so funny. It's why I truly have such an appreciation for guys like you, Luke, and the other guys that really are pioneers. Everything that we do or everything that we think we know about online direct response marketing all has its origins from guys like you, from the guys that were doing it in the real world. When it was a lot harder, when you had to get people to send in checks or money orders and there were no, like you couldn't just boom, boom, boom with your credit card. You have an appreciation for how much easier we have it today than… It's funny because I used to have a little chip on my shoulder about that years ago because you hear people talk about how this won't work or that won't work. Or you have people come out and sell courses and talk about how they were, especially some of the fakers early on, probably eight, nine years ago. They talk about how something would work and you'd say, well, you didn't have to put your money up to prove or find out whether that worked. You literally had some of these free things in. I'm all for free traffic if you can get it. The very first time I was ever introduced to JV traffic, hopefully this doesn't upset any Apple cards, but the first time I heard about it… I used to hear people talk about, like this was back in probably 2002, 2003 when I first started marketing online, people would talk about, oh, you can just set up a JV. Again, people would say, well, Luke, you're coming from scarcity, but my answer was always, I'm not giving them access to my list. What are you, nuts? Because I grew up in an era where you had to pay to build that list. You had to pay a lot of money to build that list. You didn't just let it out to people and give their product an endorsement. The world's changed a little bit and I've done a couple of JVs over the years, so it's like I've sort of relaxed the idea a little bit, but a lot of that came from having to pay a lot of money to develop your customer base. I guarantee you, if you talk to a lot of the underground internet marketers who do tons of media buys, they are not real quick to JV with you, because they're like me, they had to spend a lot of money to build that list. Yeah, and so you appreciate what goes into generating your customer list, acquiring a new customer. You brought up a pretty fascinating topic, man, that I want to just go down for one second, because I think that it really will be valuable for everybody on here. Knowing what you know about direct response, direct mail, and making it work like when there's money on the line and whatnot, what do you see as the biggest difference in thinking between the way the average online marketer thinks about their marketing? That's an easy question. Sure. Unless there's more, but that's a good question. The biggest difference I see is something that I'm trying to train myself to be more like. I'm trying to train myself to be more like an internet marketer in this way. Here's what it is. They're very, very quick to put something out that is not perfect, whether it's the sales letter or the email sequence or whatever. Because we had to spend money every time we did that, we did not send something out half cocked like, well, write a letter quickly today and let's just throw that in the mail. No, no, no. That would never happen. I think that there's probably a happy balance between, I mean, it would take eight, nine, ten weeks to write a sales letter. Edit, after edit, after edit, after edit. Okay, let's test this now. I'm not saying that's the right way, but I'm also not. I don't believe that the right way is throw something together really quick. You know what, it doesn't matter, it's JV traffic because you're disrespecting your JV partner. Or if you're spending money on your own traffic, you don't have to spend much to find out if it's a loser or not. But I think that the quickness to put something out that's not quite dialed in is probably the biggest difference. Yeah, and so where do you see that fine line? And I agree with you. I think that there's definitely a balance between, you know, like write speed, you know, like today, you know, you've got to be quick to the market. Speed is a critical element of business today. And so where do you see that balance? How do you, or how are you striving to find that balance between getting stuff out there, getting sales letters out there, getting offers out there so that you can test them, see response, and that sort of thing, but yet not do it too soon so that what you're putting out in the market is subpar? I think that the biggest help for me is opening up and letting my wife know what I'm committed to because she will make it happen faster. Very cool, I like that. I mean, that's just the truth, you know, because if I don't want to be pushed, I just don't say it out loud. So you have a mechanism though to deal with an app that allows you to kind of sneak out and you just don't tell anybody. When your wife says, hey honey, what are you working on? Nothing. You know, as long as there's still cash flow, we're all good, you know. But yeah, so the bottom line is I think that I'm probably not a good example of that because I'm still working on that speed thing. I've got a couple of really close friends that I'm sure you've got the same thing if you're not one yourself who can implement so fast. I can remember being on a conference called Three or Four People and we were talking about five or six different ideas. One of the people on the call was Ed O'Keefe, I don't know if you know Ed, but you want to talk about speed to implementation. Ed is the king of that. The next morning we were on chat and he goes, yeah, check out the landing page and I'm like, landing page, what are you talking about? Well you know, the stuff we talked about last night and when we got done with the conversation, it was like midnight. And this is like nine in the morning, he's already got the landing page and I'm like, see him. I'm still thinking about what the headline would be. That is funny man. Some people are just really fast. Yeah, yeah. And so, well how do you decide when it, because this is a question that I frequently get asked and I know that a lot of marketers struggle with. I don't know if we would call it this whole perfectionism. I don't know if I'd go that far, but the question is, how do you decide when a promotion, when a sales letter, when a video sales letter is good enough to go out? What kind of criteria do you use to assess that? Okay, so it depends on the piece, but I've got a group of people that I've known for a long time whose opinions I trust. Now again, it's still not like putting in front of your customer, but they are people whose opinions I trust and usually if I get feedback when I share something, whether it's a sales letter, a postcard, a video. If I get the feedback, and this didn't come from me, but I've experienced this over and over again, if they go, oh, yeah, that's not bad. Or they're scared to tell you, or they give you a couple of pieces of critique. I know we've got a problem. If they come back and go, holy crap, how do I buy this? It's ready to go. You've got to put it in front of people that would be your target market. I can remember having one sales letter, my first real big hit online in 2002, I had, I mean, we're talking about my mother-in-law, people that would never, ever be my target market, or I wouldn't think would be my target market. We're reading it and wanting to buy the software I'm selling. I knew we had a mass market hit. Now, and so let's talk about that for one second. And so do you have, let's kind of turn, you know, take a right turn for one second and talk a little bit about. So when you're creating, when you're writing a sales letter, when you're creating a script for a VSL or something like that, creating a direct mail piece, whatever it may be. What kind of process do you use in terms of editing? So you write really quick, get it out of your head, get it down on paper, so to speak, then go back and edit, and how many times do you edit, and is there a process that you use for that? Yeah, so the very, the thing that I spend the most time on, writing a sales letter actually is fairly quick for me, even though sometimes if people that know me would say, well, that's not true, I saw you take eight weeks on that letter. The letter itself is pretty easy. The editing process is fairly quick. The part that takes a long time is finding the right hook. That hook is everything. And some people are really gifted at being able to come up with that stuff, like off the top of their head, or they can borrow it from other places. But, you know, in the, man, I sound old when I say this, but in the old days, you didn't have as much or the size of swipe files that you have these days. And so you couldn't just quickly go online and look at either competitors or markets that were similar, but not competitors, and kind of get an idea for which direction to go. I mean, you had very limited access to what your competitors were doing. You could see stuff in infomercials, and you could see stuff in magazines, and things like that. But for the most part, you had to come up with that hook yourself. And so once you have that, the letter, the letter, the VSL, the sales message pretty much writes itself. I come from a sales background, so once I've got that hook, I can, I sit down to write the letter the same way I pretend, and sometimes I actually do it without pretending, I pick up the phone and pretend that I'm actually selling it to somebody, and that's what's getting written down. As I'm working out the pitch, I'm usually doing it with old sales friends that I have where I'll call them and say, hey, I want to pitch you. And they'll say, okay, go ahead. And I just start going. And the phrases that come out over and over again end up in the letter, and the flow that you get comes out, that ends up in the letter. That's the process I use. I don't know if that's unique or not. And now, is there a process? Like so, once you've got it down on paper, for example, or once you've got the initial version out of your head, down in print, so to speak. How do you, what is your, do you have a specific editing process that you use to go back and make sure that you've got it dialed? Yeah, but a lot of that is just reading through it, reading through it, reading through it. And again, because I come from a sales background, it's literally reading it out loud. Don't read it in your head. Read it out loud as you would be doing a sales pitch. And I guess, you know, after reading a million, zillion books, you just, you know, you know, if it's right or not, or like, you know what, that was kind of clunky. We need to fix those few sentences. You know, when that was coming out of my head, that sounded different than the way it looks now when I read it. Let's fix that. But most of the time, the ideas stay, and it's really just a matter of changing the language in around a little bit. Using a different word or making it clear if I go and I pitch it to somebody and they say, well, what do you mean by that? I know we've got a problem, so let's fix that. Is that what you mean? Yeah, pretty much. I mean, you know, I love to, I'm always interested in hearing, you know, the, you know, I know that, you know, I learned very early on the whole idea of, you know, when you're writing, you know, to write. Don't try to edit and write at the same time. Get the ideas out of your head, right, because they use two different sides of your brain. And so, as well, try to write quickly. And so, when I'm writing a script or when I'm writing, you know, a sales letter, I'm trying to get the ideas and concepts out of my head without any kind of internal or external editing. And then I go back and I'll edit later after I let it sit for a little bit so that I'm doing it with fresh eyes. Some people I know will go back and they might, you know, they might have, you know, three different editing paths that they do. Once over might be looking for things that they can cut out to really tighten the copy, cutting out senses, shortening up the senses. Then they might go back through once more and look at the verbs and look to see if there's, and so I'm just always fascinated by other copywriters and what their process is for really going back and tightening up the copy. But that was awesome. Video has changed that a lot for me. You know, back in the days before being able to deliver it on video, you know, you're right. If it had to be written down, I would really worry about getting everything tightened up. But these days, if I'm doing, and for a VSL, I guess it would be the same thing because it's still written. But if you're doing like a lot of the videos that I've done that I've had a lot of success with have been, you know, talking head to head, you know, like, you know, live somebody on camera, you know, me being on camera. With that, you can actually get away with some filler copy. And as a matter of fact, if you don't have some, it's going to sound kind of fake because you're not real. But if I'm going to go deliver it, like, you know, for the Post-Card Profits course, we had a video that was about nine minutes long altogether. About two and a half minutes of that was testimonial. So about seven minutes of that was me scripted. And I had person after person after person, person after person going, man, that was so amazing. You did that, it looked like you did that all in one take. And all, you know, all, you know, that was scripted, that was written, and I memorized it like an actor in a movie. And it looked like I was just coming up with it off the top of my head. But the people who try to do that, and they really do just try to do it off the top of their head, you can tell, you know, unless you're just an absolute consummate salesperson, it's nearly impossible to do that and have it be something you could put out in mass and have it work. Yeah, yeah, I want to say one more thing and then I want to respect your time and get you out of here. And I want to give everybody an opportunity to learn how, where they can get more info from you and learn from you and whatnot. But I couldn't agree with you more, I think that, and I really got to credit my friend Rich Sheffrin for really proving this to me, that everything we do today in terms of our sales videos, you know, VSLs, is scripted. Every word, every phrase, every chuckle, every pause, everything. And so, you know, one of the most time consuming things, people have asked, you know, how long did I work on this launch leading up to the opening of the Six Figure Funnel Formula Training Program. And the most time consuming part was the scripts for the four videos. So those videos that were 20, 22, 23 minutes, 18 minutes, whatever they all were, every single word, every single phrase, every single pause, every single chuckle, every single thing in there was fully scripted and scripted to be delivered in a certain way. And of course, the guarantee is you can go through the entire program, take a full 30 days, go through everything, see the whole thing for yourself, put it into action, see that it does everything that I told you that it will, and then decide. And if it's not for you, no biggie, just shoot an email over to our support staff and as long as it's within 30 days of today, you'll get a full refund. And so you'll be good to go. And so I want to talk a little bit about email, specifically email copywriting, starting with just, what is your general view of the role that email plays in an online business? So let's start with that. Well, I mean, the answer to that question is what I've always believed since I started doing email marketing is, I mean, the most profitable thing you could ever do on the internet is, I believe, is email marketing. I mean, where it's the thing that allows anybody to become self-sufficient once you're able to build your own list and be able to send emails because it's the real true push button. I mean, everybody looks for these whiz bang hacks or loopholes or whatever. I mean, there really is no loophole or hack. The fact of the matter is, I mean, email marketing to me is the only push button method out there to even push button traffic method for sure. And so what would you say, just let's get this, you know, the elephant in the room out of the way, just for some of the marketers that might be even thinking about it. So what would you say to the marketer or to the individual that says that, you know, email is becoming less and less effective because of spam, because of the amount of competition and noise. And so it's becoming less and less valuable as a marketing tool. What would you say to that? They're full of shit. But that's probably not what you hear. But the fact of the matter is, I mean, anybody can cry about it as much as they want. There's nothing changed. I mean, if you go look, I mean, you can go look at all the statistics, everything else out there. I mean, they were doing huge, you know, everybody moves over to this idea of social media and everything else when, you know, you go look at people, maybe they're advertising budgets going over there, but the fact is the most profitable things they're doing is the email marketing. Now, you know, if you can't adapt or if you can't learn, I mean, the biggest thing I've learned over the years is, you know, there is it adapting. I mean, heck, you know, when I first started, we would just push, you know, send and hell, you'd make sales for a week. You know, you never had a mail. I mean, the frequency has changed since then. I mean, there's a, you know, even the concepts. I mean, heck, when I first started, we were sending emails, you know, before the Can Spam Act and all that stuff, we were sending sales letters as the email. The links people were clicking on was the link to go order. You know, I mean, the, I mean, we, you just got to adapt throughout whatever is going on. I mean, and the fact of the matter is, is, you know, there's different things you could do to be able to, you know, get past that. You know, and everybody's got to decide what game they're going to play. I mean, if people are, if we look at any internet marketer, most in 99, every internet marketer I know, there's not that many people I know made money, you know, all their money from SEO. I mean, most of these guys that are doing all their money, they're doing it through email marketing. So it's not going to die. When you, you know, a day without emails, like a day without sunshine, it's going to happen. You know, it's just a matter of adapting to it. And so, so you mentioned, okay, so you mentioned frequency, you mentioned a day without email, like, right? And so, what frequency do you recommend for your house list? I'm talking about, let's say, you know, you've got a house list of prospects that have gone through your front end funnel and haven't bought. You're mailing them once a day, twice a day, once a week, three times a week every day. What frequency do you use? Every list, I mean, every list is treated differently that we have. I mean, our customer base, you know, typically we're only sending them emails whenever there's something that we've got going on. Or if there's something that's, you know, doing a favor for a friend or something like that. But typically, you know, you've got to protect them. It's a matter of, you know, from that perspective. If they're more of a lead, they haven't done anything. You know, our goal is to get them to move them to become a buyer and move that buyer list as much as possible. But the other, but also, it's, you know, we were selling affiliate stuff to that list. We are mailing in that list. We actually are. Our frequency has gotten pretty high. I mean, in affiliate marketing, especially when you're running a strictly affiliate marketing business model, you know, you're running off of numbers, which is EPC. So it means earnings per click. So you've got to get clicks to make that happen. One way to increase your clicks, your daily click count, is by increasing the frequency of your emails. Now, you know, I just, you know, everybody can, people could cry about it, you know, when it comes to that. We can get more subscribers, you know, or we can, you know, get more clicks by, you know, get more clicks by, you know, doubling up our mailings or things like that. I think, you know, we do, Max, we do two a day. I mean, for that one list, you know, that's more of a prospect list or more of a let's call it a lead list. You know, once we move and we call it to the middleman, we actually have three. We have the one list, we have a middleman list, which that one is whenever we find something really hot or whenever I'm doing something, they get, they get communicated with. And then the other list is I protect them like crazy. It's, you know, it's not a, it's not a numbers game with them. It's a protect them. Don't, you know, treat them, you know, treat them like gold because they are, they've already invested in you and they're going to invest more. So every relationship is a little bit different. And, you know, most people don't understand. The one you don't think will work is the one that's going to work. I mean, I honestly, I swear to you, we do five, we take five radically different subject lines every time that we mail. Now, I wanted to make a clear understanding for everybody to understand is the fact that we're not looking, when we're looking at our subject line, it's the open-to-click ratio is what we're staring at. Our numbers are based upon the open-to-click ratio, meaning, you know, every subject line, when I pick or choose a winner, to me, remember, EPC is about earnings per click. I've got to see, it doesn't matter, subject lines can dictate the clicks. Now, I can argue with people and I've argued with people in the dame form about this, because I know I'm right and I know they're full of shit, I'll fight all day long on it, until I decide I'm ready to let it go or I just whatever, let them just think whatever they want to think. I hate to say this, but I did a product that was like a thousand and one subject line split test. The funny thing is, and I laugh because I would much rather find out about clicks, me, I don't care. I was like open rate getting, you know, and it was more to me, it was a joke, because I don't give a shit about opens. All I care about is clicks. You know, if people open up, I mean, and I was telling somebody in my office, like, this is hilarious, because I'm fighting with people and people don't realize that a subject line can dictate your clicks. You can have the lowest open rate email, so an email can have the lowest open rate, but have the most clicks. And everybody thinks, well, if the open rate is, you know, if a higher open rate means a higher click rate, no, that's bullshit. And if people think that, then they're not testing, because the fact is, you know, think about this. If you send an email like, dude, right, you know, I write, oh, best open rate subject line. Send an email like, dude, and it's like, hey dude, check this out, you know, whatever, yeah, I might get some clicks. But it will get open, because everybody's going like, what the hell is he calling me dude for? But, you know, but if it's like, buy my shit, right, part of my language, but I'm just going to say it this way. So you've got a subject line, dude, and you've got a subject line, buy my shit. Well, when somebody opens, buy my shit, you know, they know they're going to go in there and click a link and buy my shit, you know. I'm going to tell them, like, hey, this shit's really good, go buy it, you know. So you're going to get, well, maybe more clicks, but if not, the click, the ratio is going to be good, and I guarantee you, you're going to make more sales off of the, you know, more, you know, buy my shit email, and you will have to do the email. That's funny, man. And so, and it's true, and in some cases, you know, you might have a subject line that is, generates an extremely high open rate, and not only does it generate an extremely low click-through rate, but it also generates a tremendous number of opt-outs, because people feel like you just, you know, calm them into opening the email. So what is the specific number that you look at? Is it strictly click-through rate? Is that how you judge? It's the ratio of opens to clicks that you judge. That's interesting, man. Okay. Yeah, I mean, a lot of our stuff, if I'm looking, I do pay attention to that number, and, you know, so I can actually, you know, I mean, we do pay attention to that number. I mean, yeah, it is the click-through rate, I mean, the fact is, is, you know, one subject line, it's what subject line is going to produce the most clicks for me, you know, and if we even track it even more, depending, if it's my offer, I mean, of course, we're going to do what subject line is creating the most sales for me. But when it's an affiliate offer, we can't get that deep into it. So, but, you know, I mean, and that's what people, that's what kind of my point is, and knowing testing even that one thing is knowing that, you know, one subject line can create more clicks, and you can take the same email, every, you know, like, say, five radically different variations of subject line, same email, and you're going to see radically different results, and it's, it's, it's crazy. I mean, it's like, you know, the one, let's say you have one with really huge open rates and, you know, really low clicks, you know, and they see one with really low open rates, but really high clicks, compared to the rest, you know, when you're comparing it. And so, and then that one also could be the one that's getting the most sales. So, you know, making, we make decisions based on that. I mean, we do, we do split test, actually, ten subject lines a day. So, and so I've been seeing this, I mean, it's not a trend, it's true. So, you know, the funny thing is, is we make it a game. So, when we're split testing, we make it a game, and it's like, oh, which one do you think is going to win, you know, and then, you know, the funny, that's why I said at the beginning was nine times out of ten, the one I don't think will ever win is the one that wins, you know. There is no rhyme or reasons, it's not logical, it's inlogical, it's just crazy. That's funny, man. That really is. Counterintuitive is what I'm trying to say, yeah. Yeah, yeah. And I think, you know, it's funny, I think you bring up such a great point that everybody really should keep in the forefront of their mind, and that is always making sure that the goal, the target that you're aiming for is the right target, right, that you're not like, you're not focusing on a target. He said to John, you know, when he was about to swing, and John was like, you know, I'm aiming for the fairway or whatever, I don't remember the exact story, and the dude said, well, you're aiming for the wrong spot, you should be aiming for the hole, like that's where you ultimately want to go, and, right, and it's, you know, it's, it's, when it comes to writing the subject, writing the subject line, remember that you're not crafting a subject line in a vacuum, that at the end of the day, it's not just about, like, open rate, but ultimately, and this goes back to something that we were talking about earlier on in, in our, in our stream today, and that is that, you know, at the end of the day, it's all about ROI, it's all about the money. The only thing that matters is return on investment, and so at the end of the day, you want to make sure that you're not, like, testing subject lines, let's just say, and choosing a winner based on open rate, that's like, insignificant in the scheme of things. Yeah, exactly, the key is that you cannot know the English language very well, that's the key, and so, I love it, man, and so, all right, so let's start to bring this to a close, so that you can go back and enjoy yourself with the kids, watching the movie, so, what about the actual body copy when you are writing email for your prospect, any kind of, any advice that you have in terms of length, in terms of links, are you including the raw link in there, are you linking words and phrases, how do you choose what to link, and kind of any advice in those arenas? Yeah, I mean, typically, nine times out of ten, we are linking, we are actually giving them the raw link, nine times out of ten we are, and that could be just for no other reason than laziness, I mean, just to be true, I mean, we don't typically test if we just add it in there, find that it works, I mean, especially when you are dealing with a lot of the people, I mean, maybe they don't realize, you know, you can actually click the words, but the fact is, when we are writing emails, I mean, I am always looking for, I mean, I sit there and it depends, if I am writing an affiliate offer, I will give you the quick easy way we do it, if it is an affiliate offer, especially with them, you know, I am going in, I am reading the headline, seeing how I can turn the headline into an email, or I am going in and reading the first few lines of the copy, and seeing if I can snag that and using that in email, because I want it to be as congruent with whatever I am mailing to as much as possible, and I am like, hey, you are going to find this absolutely amazing thing on the next page, and that is great, and that is probably what it says sometimes too, I mean, the shorter the better, but if you think about it, a lot of times people are using the copywriters for, especially an affiliate side of things, the copywriters spending most of their time writing the headline and all the other stuff, so if I can utilize that headline, if I can utilize the first couple of words in that sales letter to my advantage by using it in the email, or actually making that email, actually we will do it, that right there is usually how I come up with the first email, and if it works, then I might get a little fancier, but the shorter the better, in a click environment when it is a good, we are doing lower ticket items, and a higher ticket item is going to need a lot more, probably a little bit longer, going to have to do a little more pre-sale, because it is just going to need that, but lower ticket items when it has got a good EPC, crap, my rule of thumb is, if I send an email and I get you somebody to come to your page, it is your job to sell them, so my job is to get them to you, as long as I get the right people to you, which they should be, I mean, if I am picking the right offers to mail, and I am getting the right people, because that is who is on my list, then when I get them to you, it is your job to sell them, and if you can't sell them, then you suck, you know, I mean, Either they suck, or you suck at selecting affiliate offers, one or the other. Yeah, it is one or the other, yeah, yeah, I am actually pretty good, I know what my list reacts to, and I know what my list does not react to, and I know which list I can go to for certain things, we actually joke around sometimes, because I have a guy in the office that mails, I give him permission, Actually, the funny thing is, when we talked about earlier, we were talking about my customer list, my customer, I have a guy in my office that mails from me, but I did tell him one thing, I said, you cannot cross the line, you can touch the one list, you can touch the middleman list, but you cannot cross the line and touch my customer list or I will kill you, When I say protecting, I am protecting it, but my other list, my guy will hit me up, and I will say, oh, you think this is awful work, I said dude, I will tell him, that looks like a great offer to the middleman, mail it to the middleman, but if they don't react, don't mail it to the other one, because you are going to be wasting an email, So, and in 9 times out of 10, I am right, but I have been doing it long enough, and when you are picking new offers every day, you kind of get it honed in really well, just by the look and feel of it. Yeah, yeah, and so very cool man, there is one question, and then I want you to share with everybody how they can learn more from you, where they can go to learn more from you, and then I want you to go back to having fun with the kids, and so Dave asks, what is a good typical email length? Well, for the spam filters or for getting clicks, no, I mean, we just sent one out, I just wrote one for my guy, I mean, email length, people aren't going to like this, but we might have, not that long, I will try to figure out how many lines long, but it is not going to be the best converting ones that we are sending out, about one, two, three, four, six, seven, you know, about 15 lines, I mean, I am talking with spaces, so I might have one line short, and then about 15 lines long, you know, it is probably about the normal length of an email that we are sending out, so it is about that big, you know, it is really short. Oh, it is short, yeah, it is short in a few things, and how many links are in that length email that you send out? One, yeah, I mean, it is typically going to be one, I mean, everybody talks about, oh, there is more, you know, the PS and everything else, but now if you do it short enough, then we will do it. Now, here is one thing most people don't realize when you are doing it, that first line, you know, we talk about the subject line, you know, but also the first line, you know, when people are actually viewing, and going over to see if they are going to open it up, that first line in your email really does well, and a lot of times, I try to get the line, the first line to kind of relate as much as possible to the subject line, and what I find that really does help, because, you know, they know that when they open it up, it is congruency too, it is a message, you know, it is matching the message you said in your subject line. So, you know, I might say, hey, you know, this free, you know, download this free thing, and then it might be saying, hey, I just got this new free gift for you, you know, so it is really congruent, really the same. You know, first line and subject line, because some people can read that in their browsers. I mean, in their, you know, adaptations. Yeah, absolutely, man. And so awesome.
