And so you are able to collect that data as well as do a whole bunch of other stuff. And I want you to understand the nuances of doing that. Now, while we are waiting, we have got ten minutes. I want to talk to you a little bit about, I got a question last night. I was on, you know, I had like five webinars or something crazy yesterday. And one of the webinars, I think it was with Don Crowther late last night, if I remember correctly. Somebody asked a great question about email marketing. And I am going to give you the answer that I shared last night. And I want you to file it away in the back of your mind. Some of you, it might be a little bit advanced for some of you, but regardless, it is the right answer. It is the answer that I gave. And the question was, the question that was asked was, how do I email my house list? How do I email my list? And email them regularly. Email them promotions for products without pissing people off. Right? So how am I emailing promotions and campaigns? Like I have got a series of three emails that I want to send out about a particular product that we are releasing or that we have now. And I want to send it out, but how do I do it without pissing off my entire list? And the answer was really in the question. And the answer is that you do not send that entire three step campaign to your entire house list. Now when I say house list, when you heard Luke earlier talking about house list, for our purposes right now. Your house list is, for our, right now, your house list is your list of prospects. These are individuals that have gone through your funnel. They did not buy, let us just say, it could have been more than one funnel. They have come in and now you have got this list of prospects. I am not talking about your customer list. I am talking about your pool of prospects. And so there is a certain communication process, communication system, communication approach that we use. You heard one communication approach or one emailing approach, if you will, from Matt. Well I am going to give you a little bit of a different perspective on how to do it. So let us say we are going to release a product on, I think the example I used last night was how to repair your marriage. Right, so I now have this new product on how to repair your marriage. Let us say I have an audience of individuals that consist of, you know, individuals that are interested in romance and sex and dating and writing. So not everybody is married and not everybody is having problems in their marriage. Therefore not everybody is going to be interested in a course on how to repair your marriage. And so I do not want to just send a multi-step email campaign to the entire list because a lot of the people on the list might not be interested. And so I want to identify the individuals on the list that are interested. And then I want to market to those individuals. It is almost very similar to what we do with the traffic activation page. It is almost very similar in that it becomes a two-step marketing process. And so here is what I mean. So you have got your entire house list. You have got your own list of prospects that are, you know, a variety of different issues all related under one umbrella to, you know, having a better love life, romance, sex, blah, blah, blah, dating. And so before you release this three-step campaign that sells people on the product for repairing their marriage, what you would do is you would send out a blog post. You would send out a video. You would send out some piece of information that they can consume that has to do with the topic of repairing your marriage or improving your marriage or, you know, fixing a broken marriage, right? It could be like a set of blog posts. It could be a video. And so you are going to send out an email to your house list. And you are going to give them a link in there to go and watch the video. Go and read the article. Go and listen to the audio. Listen to the interview. Whatever it may be. You are using that piece of content strictly for the purpose of identifying the prospects on your list, in your house list, that are interested in that topic. Then what you do is the individuals that click on the link to go and read the article, to go and watch the video, those individuals get put into a subsequence. They get put into a subsequence where now, over the next three days, those individuals, the individuals that identify themselves as being interested by clicking the link in the content email are going to get the series of emails. The people that did not click on the link because it's not of interest to them. It's not something that they have any interest in. Those people are not going to get put into that subsequence. They are going to continue to stay in your house list and get whatever email you send out the next day or two days later or three days later or whatever it may be. In essence, what you are doing is you are lead generating out of your house list. You are using specific pieces of content that you are sending out that require them to click on a link to go and view the content or read the content or get the content. By clicking on the link, that is how we say they have raised their hand and identified themselves as they are interested in. They are interested in that content. From that, we are going to plug them into a mini follow-up sequence that then introduces them to our product. If you are using a tool like Infusionsoft or Office Autopilot, it is very easy to do. If you are using something like a Webber, it is a little bit more difficult to do. It actually requires a third-party tool that is also about $20 called AW Pro Tools. AW Pro Tools over the last handful of weeks, I have spoken multiple times to Jack Bourne, the creator of that. I worked somewhat closely with him to see what ways we can use AW Pro Tools with six-figure funnel formula. Jack is in the process of preparing a special offer for AW Pro Tools that will cut the price down even more just for six-figure funnel formula training clients. AW Pro Tools allows you to do things like that. It allows you to take a prospect and just from them clicking on a link, you can put them into a different list. You can take them off one list, put them into a different list. For example, you have a house list of prospects. You send out an email about an article on how to repair your marriage. They click on it. You can immediately take them out of the house list, subscribe them to the three-day email list, the three-day email sequence that sells people on the new product how to repair your marriage, and then at the end, if they buy, they get put onto the customer list. If they don't buy, they get put back onto the house list, all fully automated. That's the way you do it. You're not just sending out the same email to everybody. That's one of the signs of a novice marketer, of an amateur marketer. It's great. I've got a list of people. In some cases, some marketers will not even view their customer base separately from their prospect base. They're like, I got a list of, it's a total of 85,000 people and I'm shooting an email out today. I'm shooting an offer out today. That's the sure sign of an amateur. Segmentation is one of the most valuable things for you to have. That's what makes the six-figure funnel formula so freaking powerful is that it does the segmentation for you. Somebody buys the customer captivation offer but doesn't take the reverse add-on. It immediately puts them into a certain chain in the amplification order responder chain that does the specific follow-up for you to get them to take advantage of that offer. If they take advantage of the customer captivation offer and the reverse add-on but not the soft add-on, it pops them in and it does it automatically. It's doing the segmentation for you automatically based on what offers your prospects are taking or not taking. If they don't convert, they get put into one sequence. Once they do convert, there's another chain and another chain and it's all fully automated. Segmentation is the key. Let me see here. I want to move this out of the way for one second because there are... One of the questions, Sharon said this is so interesting, great. It's so my house list does not get burned since I got into Todd's list. I'm learning more than I have for years. I appreciate that Sharon and I appreciate having you here for sure. Here's a question. Do you know if you can do that crazy autoresponder, optimization? It's getting late. Sorry folks. Optimization, segmentation in GetResponse because I love them for a lot of reasons. I have no idea if you can do that in GetResponse. I'm sure I would think you probably... I don't know. That's the answer unfortunately. I know that it can be done with Aweber using like I said a $20 tool like AWProTools. Of course, if you're using a more advanced tool like an infusion soft or an office autopilot, of course you can absolutely do that. That's how you communicate with your list without burning the list out. The six-figure funnel formula shows you how to do... It doesn't even show you how to do segmentation. It just sets all the segmentation up for you and automates all of the different sequencing for you. It's kind of like a cool out-of-the-box thingy. I basically did SEO, web design, e-commerce. Basically I started getting into the paid search and realized that I liked that way better because it was much more predictable. You can get in there, change things and it takes effect that day and you can see the results immediately whereas SEO and some things like that, you build a couple more links or whatever, you wait a month and you see what happens so it's not very immediate. I basically focus on Facebook traffic, Google AdWords. I run about a little over 200,000 through Facebook. Nothing too crazy but probably I've managed over about 10 million with Google AdWords. Yeah, definitely because I truly do care about everyone on this call. The biggest thing that I would say is to not give up. To not think that you're going to hit it out of the park on the first try and to just stay at it. And then also to stay at one thing, to not constantly try something and then not have success with it and then immediately jump over to something else for greener pastures that see something through to completion. And give it an earnest try. Know your numbers, know what's working and what isn't working. And I don't know exactly who says it but they say like fail fast. And that's one of the reasons why I really like PPC is that you can drive a lot of traffic and see what's happening very quickly. So don't give up and don't jump around to too many different things. Stick with something, see it through for a certain period of time. Like I call it your due diligence. Give it your due diligence, really give it a try and just don't give up. Keep going after it. And here's the thing is that like I said, when I got started in 2001, I didn't know anybody that could help me. And so I felt alone. I didn't have people that I could turn to. Like not a guy who studies this or theory, but a guy who is in the trenches here with Joe as Joe's partner. And so I want you to take advantage of that. I'm going to throw some questions at you in just a second, Ben. But the thing that I want to say is I think that what Ben said, guys and gals, is really spot on, you know, in that, you know, you can't have this shiny object syndrome where you're constantly looking for the next opportunity. You know, ironically, the first person who really opened my eyes to that whole way of thinking and the guy who's really credited for it is Rich Sheffrin, right? Who you guys are going to hear from a little bit later. You know, Rich was the guy that said, you know, to me very early on, and it's why I ended up paying him thousands of dollars to have him coach me from a business perspective. As he said, you know, you've got to stop being tactical and you've got to start being strategic. You know, when you're strategic, you know, as it relates to first generally on a global level, when you're being strategic as it relates to your business, it is all about first and foremost, is this going to drive my business model forward? Like, you know, you don't look at sales letters. You don't look at, you don't watch video sales letters unless you're doing research or unless you're looking for, you know, swipes and that sort of thing. But you don't entertain things that are not within the strategy of your business model. You don't look at it or entertain it because it's an opportunity, right? It's an opportunity to make money. It's a new income stream. People are killing it now with Kindle books and so let's run over there. That's not being strategic, right? Rich said, right? Like, that's being an opportunity, an opportunity seeker. And, you know, so you've got to be strategic, strategic in even what you entertain. And I think what Ben said is so on the money in that, and I heard somebody say it like this, you know, you've got to focus, right? And focus is follow one course until successful. Follow one course. If you decided that you're going to be an information marketer and you're going to have your own brand and you're going to share your knowledge with the world to give value, you don't go exploring CPA marketing and how to make money with CPA offers. You don't get involved in how to start advertising and selling products at wholesale on eBay or how to, you know, get into the import, export business. You know, that's not being strategic. So you've got to stay focused. Don't filter out all the other things that don't apply to your business and your situation. And don't further the strategy that you've already accepted. That's critical. And so I think that you were spot on with that, Ben. And so let me ask you, let's start to get into some traffic. And let's talk a little bit about Google AdWords. I want to throw a bunch of stuff. I know that we've got, let me see here, we've got Ryan Lee who's coming on at 9.45. So we've got 30 minutes. And so I want to go at lightning pace and ask you a bunch of questions to get a bunch of great stuff and share a bunch of great stuff with the list. And so Google AdWords. And so let's talk generally. What's the key to making Google AdWords work today for the typical direct response marketer? So, you know, there was a point in time when, you know, like I got totally booted from Google AdWords because we were using pure, unulti... What's the word I'm looking for? Unadulterated squeeze pages. Like pure, you know, and so we got booted. And I know today that the terms of service a little bit, a little bit more lacks. But so what are some of the things that the marketers need to know direct response marketers to use Google AdWords for their traffic? Yeah, definitely. So just like you said, the squeeze pages, Google doesn't like squeeze pages. They, you know, that basically they... I heard somebody that got some sort of a warning from them that they were calling them information gathering pages. Okay. And so Google does not like these things where you're only providing one route which is filling out a form or calling a phone number. So the way that you can combat that is by having some links on the page. And basically those links should go to deeper areas of the site. Now it doesn't mean that you, you know, you can still do it within a landing page. You don't have to take them to your main site. Okay. You do want to have them stay focused and have them take that action that you want. But like for example, a restaurant, you know, if you're looking for a restaurant, some things that, that, you know, might come to mind are the menu, right? So you would want a link that goes to the menu. And it doesn't even have to go to a different page. It could even just open a light box on that page. Another thing is, is that you might want to book a reservation. So what you want to do is, is you want to think about the things that, that people would want to go to when they land on that page and try to give that to them, right? That's what Google's all about. When you go to Google, you type in movies in your zip code. You don't even have to click on a page. They display them right there for you. So what you want to do is, you know, give the user what they're looking for as fast as possible. That's what Google likes. You want to lead them to another page, but you want them to take that action. So some of the things that you can do to combat that are to also make the page longer. So instead of it being necessarily going to different pages, make that page longer and have those different sections on that page. Because Google knows how much they're scrolling on that page. They also know how much time that they're spending on the page and whether or not that they're bouncing. But the main thing to be successful is that you have to fulfill your promise, okay? And that goes from the keyword to the add to the landing page. And so all three of those things have to be in sync and you have to fulfill your promise. If you're not doing that, then, you know, then Google's going to see that users don't like your page. Now, I have made up two golden rules for Google, okay? And these are the things that I really like about Google AdWords as opposed to SEO. So the first golden rule is whatever makes Google the most money. Okay? That's the first one and I'll get into these in a little bit here in a second. The second golden rule is whatever provides the best user experience. So in terms of the number one rule, whatever makes Google the most money, if, you know, everyone thinks it's about bids and things of that nature, but it has more to do with quality score and that gets into their secret sauce. But essentially, if Google shows your ad a thousand times and they show your competitors at a thousand times, if Google makes $10 with your ad and only makes $5 with your competitors' ad, then Google's going to favor you because they're making more money with you. Meaning that they're going to reward you with higher placement because you have a better click-through rate, which is making them more money. In other words, if they show your ad a thousand times and another competitor's ad a thousand times and people click on yours a lot more, Google's going to make a lot more money with your ad than the competitor's ad, right? Exactly, exactly. And then the number two rule is whatever provides the best user experience and the reason is that if users aren't having a good experience and they're not going to come back to Google, so ultimately that's going to affect the number one rule. And that's why Google doesn't like the squeeze pages because they feel that their users don't like them. That their users are seeking information and they want to click around and look at things. So those are the two golden rules and I don't want to get too techy on it, but basically they look at it on an ECPM basis, earnings per thousand impressions. That's how a search engine looks at it. But in a nutshell, it's what we already covered, which is that if they're making more money with your ad than your competitor, then they're going to reward you, they're going to increase your quality score. So that's really the bend, right? That's really more of an ad issue rather than a landing page issue, right? I mean that has to do with how the click-through rate that you are getting on the ad rather than the landing page. Correct, correct. There's several different factors, but yeah, you're absolutely right. And so click-through rate, we say is about 60% of quality score, which is the quality score is Google's secret sauce and the higher your click-through rate, essentially the more money that they're making. Because remember, for us a conversion is that opt-in form or that sale or that phone call, whereas for Google they get paid every time there's a click. So to Google a click is a conversion. Okay, and so today, just to be clear on something, so today we're using a pure unadulterated opt-in page, a pure call it a traffic captivation page, which is, you know, there is no navigation under that. Will that still get you slapped today on Google? Yeah, it can, it can, but it doesn't always. What I like to do is more of like a mini site, okay, where it's like a landing page, but it's got some other pages on it. Okay, and so it's got, it's got to have links, right, navigation, can that navigation be in the footer? It can be, but Google recognizes that too, that, you know, and they know that when people aren't going down to that footer, so just having links in the footer alone aren't going to necessarily do it. They know more about what people are doing than they really people realize. And how much content do you need to have on the page? Like, I mean, you can obviously still send people to a page that's got a clear web form for, you know, for opt-in, correct? But so what, what's the minimum that you need to have on that page besides the navigation that would meet the requirements of Google, give you a good enough quality score and not get you banned? Yeah, I mean, honestly, the content on the page isn't really that important. The content on the page is important in the sense that it keeps, that if it's keeping people on that page, but I mean, you could just have a video and a couple key words. Like, people think that it's, you know, they get confused and think that it's kind of like SEO and you got to have all this content on the page for PPC to have a good quality score. And to be honest, I mean, I've seen pages that have a hundred words total, let's say, and a video and some nice imagery and the quality score is fine. So I don't really see that the content really affects the quality score that much, but something that you definitely want to have on the page other than links to other pages is a privacy policy. And especially if you're doing remarketing as Google calls it or retargeting, then you definitely want to have a privacy policy and in that privacy policy, if you look it up online, there's ways that those users can opt out of your remarketing by going to your privacy policy and you want to include that in there. Okay, very cool. And so let's talk a little bit about retargeting, remarketing. And so first of all, just take a second for everybody, since we've got a lot of new novice marketers, to just explain what retargeting is. It's a fairly new thing over the last handful of years, and so just explain it, explain what retargeting is. Yep, so all of you on this call, you've probably seen it in action and you may not have even known it. I like to call it the stalker campaign. Okay, and so if you've ever been to a website and you were surfing around the web, and next thing you know, you start seeing banners for that website that you were just on all over the web, okay, that's remarketing or retargeting. So basically what happens is that when somebody visits your website, there's a cookie that gets dropped on their machine. And what that does is, you know, then when that user is surfing around on the web, and on websites that have partnered with Google or other ad networks, that they're able to know that they were at your website, and you're able to serve ads to them. Does that make sense for everyone? You guys know that? Yeah, I think that that was a good solid explanation. So you're basically in a nutshell. You're basically the people that, let's say, come to your website, and let's say, well, let me think a step back before I say that, and why don't you talk a little bit about what is the best way for a new marketer, a marketer with a new funnel, just really launching their business. What's the best way that you've seen for them to leverage and benefit from retargeting? Where should they use it? You see that the best, most profitable way to use it is on the order form for shopping cart abandonment, or are you of the school of putting on your traffic activation page, your opt-in page to get the most amount of people. Where should the new marketer begin with retargeting? Yeah, great question. I tell you what, in Google AdWords specifically, they make it super easy, but a couple places is that it really depends upon the action that you're trying to get them to take. So, for example, if you wanted to upsell them or cross-sell them to another product, people that actually bought something, that's like your best audience. That's what they call it, an audience. We like to call it the invisible list. But what you want to do is you want to drop that pixel on all of your pages, and then you want to set up different lists for each of those pages. Google makes it as easy as just putting the URL of what that page is, and so you just want to make sure that it's targeted. So, for example, if you drop a pixel on the home page, so every person that hits on that page. Oh, and a pixel, listen, for everybody, a pixel is just a little piece of code. Basically, they give you a little piece of code, you copy it, you paste it onto a web page. Like if you're using something like WordPress, it's so simple. You can literally just copy, paste, update the page, done. So that's what Ben is. I just wanted to clarify that. Yep, exactly. And I see Aramis, Kastroff, sorry if I didn't pronounce your name right, that talking about an untargeting pixel, that's a great question, and we usually call that a burn pixel. And so basically what you can do is when you set up your different audiences, you have your whole funnel laid out, and you're dropping a pixel all along the funnel. But then what you do is when you're creating these different campaigns or lists, in Google AdWords specifically, they call it a custom audience. And so basically what you can do is you can say that the person visited the add to cart page, but did not visit the thank you page, or the confirmation page. So you still drop a pixel on that thank you or confirmation page, and build that audience for people that actually bought. But then when you're setting up your list, you're excluding those people. So real quick too, another thing is that, so you set up different lists, so you say, okay, this person visited the home page, but didn't visit the add to cart page. This person visited the add to cart page, but did not visit the thank you page, so you know that they didn't complete the sale or finish the opt-in or whatever. You want to place the code and the pixels all throughout your funnel. And then you look at your audiences for the different pieces of the funnel to figure out exactly where everyone's dropping off. And then depending upon where they're dropping off, is where you're going to want to lead them back to. So you don't necessarily always have to just go for the sale right away. That maybe you go for a trip wire. That these people added something to the cart, and now it's been seven days, and they still haven't come back and completed their purchase. So maybe you give them some sort of a free trial, and you're showing that in your banners. But another way that you can segment your lists, other than just the pages that they visited, is by time. So if somebody's been to your website in the past seven days, versus the past 30 days. So Google allows you to go up to 540 days that you can have somebody on a list. And so you want to create several different list segments for different times. And essentially what you want to do is you can potentially increase that offer over time. So you're just trying to get them to come back, and you've got a banner, and maybe it's just, hey, come back and complete your order, or you've got some branding, whatever. And after 30 days, if they still haven't come back, then strengthen that offer. Give them a huge discount, or buy one, get one free, or whatever the case may be, free trial. We did this with great success with a fishing lodge out in Panama. And basically the average cost of the trip was $6,000. And so basically we had an opt-in form on the website, and we tracked all the people that came to the website, but didn't fill out that opt-in form. Then we had a banner that said, Win a free trip. And another thing with the remarketing too, is that's important, is that you want to have, you want the banners to be branded. You want to remind them of where they were, because then that's how they'll recognize it. Otherwise, you're just going to look like every other banner. So you want to have the branding for the website, the logo. But on this particular campaign, we did the logo, Win a free trip, and a person catching a big fish. And basically this company went from, in November, is usually their slow season, and it turned out to be their best month ever. And that was with remarketing. Another quick example is for any event-based businesses. So we did remarketing for, and actually do it now as well. We've been doing it for the past couple years, for this huge music festival down in Miami. And so people come and visit the website, and then you keep showing them those banners, keep reminding them. And the other cool thing is, is that not only can you show them the banners throughout the display network, but you can also show them on YouTube. So when those same people that have visited your website are on YouTube looking at videos, that you can pop in your banner as well. So your brand continues to be top of mind for them. Another thing that I wanted to mention, because I know time's short and I'll let you ask some more questions, but another thing that I like to do is I call it hedging my bets. Because unlike you, Todd, where back in 2012, I set up a remarketing campaign through Google AdWords for essentially a bizop, a website that had a bizop. So I just thought, hey, the worst thing that they're going to do is disapprove the ads. Well, they ended up approving the ads, and then literally 30 minutes later, they suspended the account. Well, that account had been running for a long time, and we had audiences built up in that for a long time. Probably hundreds of thousands in the audiences. And all of a sudden, boom, it was gone. They suspended the account. That was it. Now fortunately, we were able to create other accounts and do different things. But in terms of hedging your bets, what I mean by that is, is don't just necessarily drop one pixel from one provider. So we like to use perfect audience for web retargeting as well as then Facebook retargeting. But so we'll drop a perfect audience pixel. We'll drop a site scout pixel, and we'll also drop a Google AdWords pixel. So that if at any point in time, we get in trouble with one of them, that we haven't lost everything. And so is there a platform for retargeting that you recommend new novice marketers start with, one over the other? Let's say they're not going to go, we're not talking about doing it the way you would do it. We're talking about just getting their feet wet to experience the benefits of retargeting. Is there a particular platform that you recommend? Yeah, perfect audience. So what I really like about perfect audience, and everybody's like, oh, Ad role or perfect audience. What I really like about perfect audience, now I don't know if Ad roles has added this yet. I mean, they should have. But the last time that I used it, it still wasn't a feature. But one of the cool things about perfect audience is that they will give you an image pixel that you can also put in an email. So anyone that opens your email, you can now also retarget to them. So Ad role does not give you that ability. Google AdWords, they do give you that ability. You can grab the image pixel and place it in there yourself. However, right now with Google AdWords, you can't retarget for Facebook. Whereas with perfect audience, you can reach Facebook, the news feed, the right side of Facebook, as well as unlike the Google Display Network throughout the web, they call that web retargeting. And then they just add in something that I haven't gotten a chance to try yet, which is Twitter retargeting. Very cool. Yeah, we ran a massive retargeting campaign. We had somebody that we consulted with and they kind of ran with it and it's pretty intense what you can do with it. And so let's kind of, let's change gears a little bit. Well, before we do, let me ask you, so with retargeting, so are you recommending just for clarity, are you recommending that marketers, even when they're first getting their feet wet with retargeting, that they start, is there a particular page in a funnel that you recommend they start with, or are you just saying paste the code throughout the website and let it create its audiences and then mark it specifically to those different audiences based on the pages that they went to. Or what is your opinion on starting by retargeting just the people who visit the order form and don't buy, like all the people that are banned in the order form? Yeah, absolutely. What I do is I drop it throughout the entire funnel and then I create lists based upon each of the pages within the funnel so that then when I'm looking, and then I'll mark it to all of them, but then I look at the specific stats and conversion stats for each of those different audiences. So I'll know, like, oh yeah, the people that visited the order form page but didn't complete the order, that our cost per conversion is $10 on those, whereas anybody that just hit the home page is $20. Yeah, I mean by all means, and it really also depends upon how many people are actually hitting that order form. So this isn't really a hard stricken rule, but there's talk around like, oh well, you've got to have a minimum audience size, and a lot of times people say like $1,000. Now I've seen things work with less than $1,000, but that's the main thing is that if you only have 100 people a month hitting your order form, that's not really enough of an audience, that you need to have more people. So I drop it throughout the entire funnel, I then create lists for each of those pages, so I'll say, okay, here's all the people that hit the home page, here's all the people that hit the order form, here's all the people that hit the thank you page. Also too, if you've got any OTOs or upsells or anything like that, who took the OTOs who didn't? And then you can show banners specifically to those guys. Yeah, that's what we did. It's pretty amazing what you can do with retargeting gang. I mean, it's basically another way for you to segment your prospects. You have the ability to, let's say you're running an extensive marketing funnel, you're running an extensive sequence of videos, you're able to retarget people based on where they left off in that sequence. So let's say even they come, they watch video one, they watch video two, but they don't watch video three. Well, then you have the ability to retarget them with ads that drive them back to watch video three. And if they watch video one, two, and three, you have the ability to follow them around the internet and see ads that remind them, like, hey, you didn't watch video three or you didn't watch video four, go watch video four. And so it's fully segmented and it's actually pretty sweet. And so we've got less than 10 minutes, probably more like eight minutes. And so tell me a little bit about how you guys are using data that you're collecting on Facebook to really just jack up your conversions and marketing results. I know that Joe was talking about the stuff that you guys were doing when Andy and I were, you know, when we were on the phone with you guys, whatever it was a week ago or something like that, two weeks ago. And so just talk a little bit about that. So first and foremost, you know, kind of share a little bit of what Data Reach does, not as a plug or a pitch or anything like that. But just share a little bit about what Data Reach does. And then very quickly talk about how you're leveraging the data that you're getting from it to make more money. Yep, definitely. So Data Reach is a WordPress plugin and it does a bunch of things that other tools also do, but something that it does that we haven't seen any other tool do is when people are connecting. So one of the things that allows you to create a Facebook Connect button. When somebody pushes that Connect button and they give you permission to get their likes and their friends and their birthday and their email and things of that nature, that what Data Reach does is it shows all of the users that have connected with that specific campaign. It shows the common interests and the common groups that they're a part of in a nice pie chart. So like let's say if a thousand people connect with you, they give you permission to get that information, then we can now see that, oh look, 25% of those thousand people are a member of this Facebook group or have liked this Facebook page or are of this gender, right? Like in the IM space, we usually see 75% or more are male. What we also see is that generally it's 35 to 54 is probably our sweet spot in terms of age. And so what it does is it allows you to gain an understanding, kind of build like that customer avatar, if you will, for you of your customers. So there's websites like Quantcast and Alexa that will kind of help give you information on demographics. And that's basically what Data Reach does is it's pulling them from Facebook. So what's their birthday, their gender, their age, what groups are they a part of, what pages have they liked. And the way that you pipe that back into your advertising is that when you're advertising on Facebook, you're generally saying like, okay, I want to target people that like get 10,000 fans, right? And that's something that you're aware of and that you know, okay? But now all of a sudden you're just targeting that one group and as these people connect, now you realize that, oh, you know what? A lot of those people also like this other page and are also a part of internet marketing super friends or something like that. And so what you then do is you gain insight on things that you may not have known before and then you then plug that back into your advertising. So okay, well let's now target, instead of targeting, you know, ages 18 to 64, let's target the sweet spot of the people that we see. Let's target specifically the gender, also to the states and the countries that where they're located. So it allows you to really hone in like a sniper and not only learn about some of these new areas, but then to then feed that back into your Facebook advertising. Now a big misconception is that people think that they're like, oh, well I'm not doing any Facebook advertising so I don't need it. Well, this allows you to really build that customer avatar and really know more about your customer than you ever have before. And just real quick too about the data reach, you know, to share what we share with you Todd, is that with the retargeting that we did on data reach, what we did was we did it with naked links, okay? So we had affiliates driving traffic to our launch and then we ran our own retargeting campaign for them, okay? But the naked link is literally just a straight link so that if somebody clicked on our banner and then went and bought, the affiliate that initially drove the traffic still got credit for that sale and we were able to drive over $30,000 of extra money in our affiliates pockets by running that retargeting campaign for them basically. Very cool man, very cool stuff man. I know that, you know, yeah man, just the information that you get from data reach in terms of the commonalities and trends among, not just the demographics of your audience but like I said, what pages they have in common or groups that they're part of and it really gives you insight into who your audience really is so that you're not assuming or guessing what the criteria is. So all right, so we've got like a minute or something like that before Ryan Lee jumps on with us. And so somebody asked a question very quickly. Let me take this off while we're waiting for Ryan. So somebody asked, what are the advantages of using Perfect Audience, a third-party solution versus just using the retargeting feature within Facebook? Well, it's actually very simple. The benefit of using something like Perfect Audience is that you get to do retargeting and remarketing on a variety of third-party websites in their network. Whereas when you're retargeting or remarketing with Facebook, you're only remarketing and retargeting to them on Facebook. So Perfect Audience gives you this tremendous universe to do your retargeting, and the Facebook is only, like I said, for remarketing or retargeting on Facebook. So with that being said, we should have Ryan Lee jumping on any minute now. They're still a little bit under, what is it? It's still just a little bit over two hours left for you to grab your membership in the Six Figure Formula training program. And there he is, man. So I don't want to waste any time. I'll put my little earbuds on and we are going to rock and roll with Ryan. So what's up, Ryan? How's it going, Todd? Good, man. How are you, dude? I'm super excited to have you here, man. You're one of the guys that I was looking forward to chatting with, man. And I've told everybody, I don't know if it's a New York thing or Northern thing or whatnot, but man, I'm just excited to have you here, dude. I appreciate it. And it could be a New York thing. We go back a long way. I remember our first conversation was probably 13 years ago online. I mean, a long time ago, when you were in the fitness industry. Yeah, man. Absolutely. You're absolutely right. And so for everybody, Ryan is a phenomenal marketer. This guy has been around for, like he just said, for many, many years. He's generated millions of dollars. And so, Ryan, let's first of all welcome, dude. Thank you. And I appreciate the intro. And yeah, I'm ready to rock and roll. Let's have some fun. Yeah, let's do it, man. And so let's start really quick. I want to start with kind of a high level, just a big broad question for a second. And that is so, you know, you've been online, you know, many years, very successful dude. You've seen, you know what it takes to be successful. You've seen marketer struggle. You've seen other marketers break through. What is, like really, with no fluff, no sugar coating, what is it that you feel these guys and gals need to hear about what it takes to be successful with your own online business? You know, that's a great question. There are really, obviously, a lot of factors. But it really comes down to two. There's kind of the mindset and then there's the tactical stuff. And I think sometimes people just focus on the tactics. They're like, well, you know, how do I get more traffic through YouTube when they're not starting with the right stuff? And, you know, it's really a combination. I mean, the biggest things I see people struggling with are procrastination, perfectionism, just, you know, constantly adding more and more steps that are just unnecessary. And I know you're a big guy with funnels. You know, they don't have a system. They kind of just throw, they do product to product. There's no rhyme or reason. And they're not really creating a business. They're just creating lots of little products. So maybe they come out with a product and they have a little bump and they have a, maybe they take success and they make a thousand or two thousand the first week or the first day or the first month. And then it slowly starts to drop because there's, then they're off to the next market, to the next product where they're not sticking in one thing for a long amount of time. I know I'm kind of just rambling on, but, you know, there's also, I think in terms of fear, you know, I think people are scared, they're both scared of success and they're scared of failure. So what I tell my clients is just take a small step and just celebrate a very small success. And a big idea, like I'm known a lot in the industry for continuity programs. I've created hundreds of them. But so the idea of like a quote unquote membership site is very intimidating. So instead of thinking of this huge process, just start with one thing and say, okay, the first thing I'm going to do is just come up with a domain name. And you get that domain name, take that first step, register the name and go and celebrate that success. Like get away from your computer, say, you know what, today all I do is get my domain name and I'm going to go celebrate, you know, I'm going to take my wife out to a movie or I'm going to go for a run or, you know, treat myself to an ice cream, whatever it is, is your little treat and celebrate that little success and build upon that. And then the next day, maybe it's just setting up a Facebook page and the next day, and then before you know it, you start to gain some momentum. Very cool. And so, and I know we're going to get into the continuity step because we're really going to dive into that. But so just one more thing. And so what would you say to the marketer who really is struggling with that whole mindset aspect of being an entrepreneur, you know, they're struggling with whether it be procrastination or fear of whatever side of the equation. But it really truly is a mindset issue that's holding them back. What do they do about that? You know, I was blessed. My first experience right out of college, I worked in a children's rehab hospital for six years. So I saw every day just a lot of bad stuff like kids dying, these awful diseases, you know, everything from cancer to scolera derma, like the spina bifida, some of the worst things you can imagine. So it put things in perspective for me really quickly. And I think it's just about perspective. It's like, what's the worst that can happen? You know, you put a product there, out there, and it doesn't really do that well. Okay, what did you learn from it? And you've got to be able to move quickly. Is it going to kill you? You know, when you're on your deathbed, is the last thing you're going to remember? Well, 30 years ago I did that launch, and it didn't quite do the numbers I did. You know, and the other thing is what you really have to do, and I tell the entrepreneurs this all the time that I help, is you can't get to attach to your product. You can't get emotionally attached, because that's the kiss of death. You know, I have a guy who'll come in and be like, this is what I want to do, and I've been working on this idea for like three years. I'm like, well, three years, you make any sales while I made one sale. Okay, it might be, you know, let's look at what's going on, or they're doing everything, all right, maybe it's your idea, you've got to move on. No, this is what everyone needs, and this is what I want to do. I say, well, it's not about what you want to do, right? It's about what's going to best serve your audience, and it's always about what's best for your audience, because otherwise you're not going to sell anything. So, I'm known to launch a product, and I remember one time, I spent about $30,000 developing this really incredible software program, and I did a test launch to one of my lists, and it was a bomb. I think I sold one unit. It was a disaster. Immediately, I dropped it and just took the right off. I'm like, it's a loss, I'm done. I'm not going to spend the next six months saying, oh, I spent all this money, and going crazy, and that's what everyone would do. They would keep pushing and pushing and pushing, and sometimes everyone says, oh, you've got to be tough, you've got to hang in there and never give up, and that's BS. A lot of times, the first thing you've got to do is give up. Now, assuming you've done, at least put it out there and test the waters, but if it's not working, it doesn't work, and that's fine, and you have to just kind of brush yourself off, and I think that's one of my abilities, and I'm the greatest dad in the world. We talk every single day still, and he always says, that's my greatest ability, is just if I run into an obstacle, I just brush it off, I get upset for like 30 seconds, and I'm on to the next thing, and you just got to keep moving your feet. Yeah, that is awesome. Guys, for everybody, and I appreciate Ryan being so transparent and sharing what some people might call a failure, or just not a successful launch, and believe me, I've had more than I care to admit of my own. But guys, and guys, Ryan shared a couple things that is worth its weight in gold, and two things that I want to point out. Number one is this idea that you can't wait until you no longer have fear. It's not about not having fear, it's about moving forward in the face of fear. So don't think that like, let me tell you, there were lots of fears that I had with even this launch after all these years. Look, when you go public and you release anything, there are always going to be people, no matter what, that have negative stuff to say, that their whole way of building the tallest building in the city is by tearing the other tall buildings down. That's the reality, but it's not about waiting until you no longer have fear, it's about moving forward even in the face of fear. And so that is a huge lesson from Ryan right there. The second thing that Ryan said that I think is so incredibly powerful in terms of thinking like an entrepreneur is that, look, sometimes recognizing that a project is not destined for success and saying we're going to cut this project right there, that's not failing. That's making a smart decision, right? Not every project that you put out is going to be a winner. And part of being an entrepreneur is recognizing the signs of which projects need to be cut from your portfolio and which projects you should not invest more time, energy, effort and resources into. So Ryan, I think that that was awesome. Alright, so let's do it. Alright, I'm done, see ya. Hey, I'll take it, I'll take it if that's all I get. But so let's switch gears and let's talk about continuity, right? And so, you know, I'm a huge believer, always have been in continuity. I think, and just for everybody, especially even the new marketers, you know, continuity is when people are paying you on an ongoing basis, you know, sometimes referred to as till forbid until they tell you to stop. It's like your cable bill, it's, you know, it's once they sign up, they're paying you, whether it's monthly, quarterly, annually, weekly, depending on your continuity program, it's a game changer, right? I came from an industry before getting online X number of years ago where I was working in the fitness industry. I worked for a company that owned multiple upscale health clubs and it was unbelievable to see that like on the first of the month, their billing would go through and they had, you know, a hundred plus thousand dollars come in per location, like right off the bat, it was like amazing. Takes a lot of pressure off. It changes the game significantly when you know, wow, what came in this morning, takes care of our entire monthly nut, we never have to worry about it, no matter what happens the rest of the month. And so, I'm a huge believer in it. And so, let's start by talking a little bit about how does a marketer who's currently selling one-off products that doesn't yet have continuity, how should they begin thinking about even kind of just even approaching the whole subject of developing a continuity program, their first continuity program, where do they begin? Yeah, well the first thing you have to do is you have to be, you know, realistic with is your topic, does it lend itself to continuity? Because there are some topics that lend itself much better to continuity and I did a blog post years ago called Don't Force Continuity and I didn't mean force continuity where someone buys a book and they're forced into it. What I mean is don't try to create a continuity program where there really isn't. So for example, if there's something that's a very event specific, event based program, like, you know, fitness for pregnant woman, that's great, that's a great product, a great program. However, when the woman has the baby, you know, why are they going to keep paying you $30 a month for workout programs for pregnant women? It just doesn't really make sense. Now you can sell a six-week or a 12-week program, but you know, to think you're going to be getting them ongoing forever doesn't really make sense. However, you can say, well, you know, so my front end product is pregnancy for, is fitness for pregnant women, maybe your continuity program, you start thinking outside the box, what are they going to want on an ongoing basis? Maybe it's supplements, okay? So maybe there's some type of, even a multivitamin that they're going to need and then maybe it's a different type once they have the baby, but they'll still keep taking that because you build trust. Or maybe it's now, you could sell some type of post pregnancy product and, you know, pregnant, you know, fit moms. There's a lot of stuff you could think down the road, but I think sometimes getting too narrow in that focus, or you know, if the whole membership site is get fit for your 20-year reunion, well now what happens when the reunion is over, or if get fit for your wedding, or things that are very, very specific with an event, tend not to be great with continuity programs. So that's the first thing. It's really, yeah. Okay, and so let's assume that, and that's a great piece of advice. It's actually something that I talk about in module one in the six-figure funnel formula training program. So I think that that's spot on, right? Understanding like, you know, are you even operating within an arena that lends itself to continuity and to ongoing. But so let's say, let's assume that there is the ability to, right? And so now where, what's the next step for them? You know, what I like to do often is work backwards. So, you know, you kind of, and it goes a little against the grain of, you know, putting the customers first. I start to think of, okay, well what's my revenue goal? You know, what do you really want to make? And because some people may say, you know what, with this product, with this program, an extra $10,000 a month. Okay, $10,000 a month is what you want recurring revenue. Now, you can say, well if I go a low price product and it's $10 a month, I need a thousand people. You know, is that going to be realistic? How am I going to generate those thousand customers? What does my marketing look like? Well, a thousand is going to be too many. Okay, if I make it $20 a month, I only need 500 people. All right, that's not bad. If I make it $100 a month, I only need 100 people. So now you start working backwards. You start thinking, you know what, well, I think for me, you know, I think 100 people in my market and what I'm selling at $100 a month, I think that's reasonable. I think I can get to that goal. And now you start to think, okay, $100 a month, what is that going to look like? And then you start putting the pieces together. You start saying, okay, $100 a month, well, it's probably going to be more of a coaching type thing as opposed to a traditional membership site. A traditional membership site is more like bigger, more robust, lots and lots of stuff, right? A lot of, you know, two, three articles a week and videos and audios and a form and all this type of stuff. And those tend to be more like $20 type programs. But it's funny, you can have a program that's $100 a month and actually deliver less because it's more about the access to you. I mean, I have a program now that is all delivered to you on Facebook where I charge $500 a month and there's zero content. Wait a second, hang on. Before you continue, I need to hear a little bit more about this. And everybody listening, I think what you're about to hear is my next continuity program. I'm just saying. I definitely don't want to, I don't want to do that at all. I was looking forward to this the whole time, man. And I'm not even joking. So, well, let me ask you this. Before we talk about the Facebook continuity program, so how do you recommend that a marketer goes about even deciding what type of continuity program they should provide? You know, right now, I think the kind of bigger view is the trend, when I first started my first continuity program, traditional one, was back in 2001. And back then, it was more about more content, more content, more content. You know, kind of drink from a fire hose. But as the years progressed, as I've created more and more, what I'm finding is that people actually want less, like it's not about more. And people, you know, information marketers assume that, well, to membership sites, $100, I got to give them so much. Man, I need to do three live trainings a week. And what happens is you overwhelm people. And you give them all this training. And they're like, well, here are three new training products this week. Three hours. And they're like, well, I didn't even get through last weeks. And now you have three more hours. Forget it. I'm out. I got to cancel. So if anything, it's about focusing less on the quantity and focusing more on quality. So with that being said, you know, you have to start to look at, well, what is your market like? What's in the past, what's worked well with it? You know, what are your strengths as a content creator? Because I'm not even talking about a continuity program like a software program. And those are really the best kind. If you can have something that really ties in. So for example, a shopping cart. You know, with our shopping, if you're in infusionsoft and you have all your stuff in there, you're kind of locked in. It's really, Dan Kenney says that pain of disconnect. That's a real high pain of disconnect. Auto responders as well. So programs like that are just really, really good. Most people aren't going to go that route and create higher end software. So I'm talking more for information marketing. You know, if you love to write and you're a really good writer, like I mentioned Dan Kenney, he loves doing newsletters. So maybe yours is going to be a newsletter that could still be done print or digital. You could deliver it as a PDF and print newsletters still work. You don't think you have to sit there, you know, in a printing press. You know, photocopies. I mean, you could send, there are companies now like disc.com. You send them a PDF file and your customer list and boom, they send it out for you. You could look, if you like doing more video stuff, video training or screen share type videos, you could deliver it as online videos in some type of password protected area or even a DVD of the month type program. So there's lots of things you could do or you could do more of a coaching program or maybe every week it's a live coaching call. It could be one week is you, pure Q&A. One week is you with a guest. Another week can be a guest and a Q&A or hot seats or member of the month. Lots of things you can do and you have all these different pieces of the puzzle and then you just start kind of putting it together. And it's funny because sometimes you think that people, well, they just want this type of content but as the months go on, you start to see, well, they're asking for more and more of this. Let me start to shift a little bit. And I always allow myself to have a little bit of flexibility because if you make a promise with a continuity program, you better keep it. So if you say every week you're going to get a brand new live video training and every week you're going to get a PowerPoint and all of a sudden after a couple weeks people don't really want it, they're still going to hold you to it. So be careful what you lock yourself into as well in terms of continuity. But yeah, I like looking at the different pieces and figuring out what am I going to deliver. But Facebook has been really exciting lately for me. And so, okay, so before we talk about Facebook, we'll talk about Facebook in a second. So tell me, how do you determine price point for your continuity program? I kind of make shit up though. Yeah, you know, like my formula is, hmm, what do I want to charge? Yeah, well look, no matter how much research you do and data gathering, there's going to come a day you've got to just press go. And you just don't, you're never going to know 100% with 100% certainty. You know, there's kind of a spectrum where if you look on one end of the spectrum I call them more soft topics where there are more hobbies, things like knitting and gardening and painting and sculpting where those are probably going to get more of a $10 to like a 29.95 per month type. You know, in that kind of range. It's going to be very difficult to do a knitting site and charge the quote unquote 97 a month. As you start moving over to the other end of the spectrum, more harder topics, things that are more return on investment, that's, you know, whether it's stock investing in real estate or make money online, you're looking at anywhere from, you know, $20 a month to, I have one right now, that's $500 a month. So, but you can make most of the soft topics hard topics. So using gardening for example, teaching people how to do better organic rose gardens in their backyard, maybe that's $9.95 a month. However, if you now teach people how to grow their own organic vegetables in their backyard and set up shop at the local farmers market every week and make an extra $1,000 a month, now charging $100 a month, alright, now it makes sense. So really is topic specific. So, you know, I'm kind of giving some broad numbers there, but, you know, I've seen programs, I've seen programs on every level work, even as low as $5 per month. Wow, okay. Yeah. And so, okay, so let's kind of, let's talk about the... But one more thing, I would always rather though, if there's, if I'm kind of hedging between the two, I would rather be on the higher end. I would rather go a little bit higher. Because it's not that much harder in my experience to get people paying $29.95 a month versus $19.95 a month. Like, don't assume at $19.95 a month, you're going to get 50 times as many people. You probably not. So, and that extra $10 a month might not seem like that much, but if you have a thousand members, that's a difference between $20,000 and $30,000 a month. That's, you know, $120,000 a year difference. That's a big swing. That is a big swing. You're absolutely right. There's a lot more books to your bookshelf back there. That's fake. That's actually a poster. It's a poster. I can see that, yeah. So, all right, so let's talk about, I'd love to hear. So what's going on with Facebook? What are you doing with Facebook and kind of share with us, man? So Facebook, what I've been doing for about the past year and a half, I've been experimenting. And I've come up with this concept called this continuity pyramid, where there's really like three C's. There's, on the bottom level, there's content, because I've said this for years, that people are going to join your continuity program, mostly for the content, but they tend to stay for community. Okay, so content's kind of the first level. The second moving up is more of the coaching. It's the interaction with you, with the quote unquote guru or expert, and then the highest level, with the highest stick rate, tends to be community. And that's where you really, that's when you really get things humming along. And I've done so many membership sites that have its own internal discussion forums, and they've gone well with Strength Coach that I started, lots and lots of interaction. But what I found was, it was getting tougher and tougher to get people interacting in these little ecosystems. And I think it's because, you know, with Facebook and all the social media stuff, people are already at other places, and there's only so many places you can go. So I figured, well, let me try delivering this in Facebook, because when you're in Facebook and that little ding, you know, hey, Todd, you were just mentioned in here, or hey, there's another post, it's like, crack. First of all, no matter what, I'm serious, no matter, I don't care who you are, you could be freaking President Obama. If you go in Facebook and it says, being someone just mentioned, Todd Brown, you are going to click that link. Yeah, no doubt. 100% of the time. So, you know, with this group, and you start getting the interaction, people start going, and you could, you know, after playing a lot with it, figuring out you could actually deliver content within that. You don't necessarily need a membership site with a username and password and the community of Facebook. I put it all in one, because my whole philosophy in business and in life is simplicity. And if I can give people one area where they can get everything, and they don't have to worry about lost usernames and passwords, it's one less step we have to worry about administrative, people are already on Facebook for hours a day. You know, everywhere you go, you look at people on their cell phones, they're checking their Facebook page. And it just gets people involved, and it builds that community, and people build relationships, and there's, I'm just loving it as a tool. And I found, you know, Stick Rates are about double. That's what I'm looking at right now. Wow, that's incredible, man. And so, basically… We're done correctly. Yeah, sure, of course. Of course. So let me ask you, seeing that like so you're, you know, hearing how prolific you are in terms of creating new continuity programs, new groups, and having multiple continuity programs at the same time. Is there kind of a benchmark that you use in terms of how you allocate your time towards content creation to sustain these programs versus like marketing? Like how do you know when you've overcommitted in terms of, you know, like there are too many continuity programs that require too much of my time to create the content to sustain them? Like is there any guidance that you could share with that? You know, it's really not a ton of content creation. I've gotten away, the more the content models, like I've sold a bunch of those sites off with the Kindle one. I basically have one guy running it. We do a revenue share type program. So I've been getting better at now building these programs where other people can run and manage, and I do more of an overseeing type thing. So I never want to overcommit myself. And if I find myself over committing, I immediately ratchet it back. But my typical day is, you know, I don't know if you know, I love working from Starbucks. That's just like my creative space. So every morning I get up, I go right to Starbucks. I work from there for about, I don't know, three hours or so. And that's like my marketing time. I do my blogging, all of my intensive, my copy, my content creation. I write for entrepreneurs. So like all these different things I get done. And then the afternoons are more my flex time. That's if I do coaching calls, or I'll go on the Facebook group and answer questions, or do interviews. I get interviewed a lot and do podcasts, things like that. But that's pretty much how I do it. The hard core content creations, the morning during my creative time, the afternoon is a flex time. But I mean, essentially I could really be done by about 11 o'clock in the morning. Three hours of intense work is really all I need to run the business. That's great, man. But when you're open and transparent, and you're not acting like, oh, I'm Superman and I could do everything, people understand. Like I'll say to my, because I have a great relationship with my groups, I'll say, hey guys, just a heads up and take you to vacation this week, so I'm not going to be on at all. And people don't have a heart attack. And if anything, they're like, great Ryan, you deserve it. Enjoy. As opposed to disappearing and never telling your list or your group anything, and hiding, and trying to check in, because you don't want people to think you're on vacation. That's the worst thing to do. Just people know you're human. And if I don't feel well or my kids are sick and I can't get off for a day, I'll email and say, look, one of my kids is sick, I'm home, I'm taking the day off. People are like, all right, great, we understand. Yeah, that's awesome, man. I'm so in line with you in that arena. I think authenticity is so critical today. And I think not only it's critical because they want to see the human side faking it, putting on a mask, putting up a front like you are, something you're not is, first of all, I can't imagine living every day like that. That's got to be a brutal monkey on your back. And frankly, look, people appreciate and want somebody who is authentic and who's got bruises and bumps and deals with everyday things, like not feeling well, like vacations, like sick kids. And so I think that's a great piece of advice. Yeah, we're human. People like that. I mean, they just want to connect. And I've just never been a bullshitter and I just want to just be real. And if something's going great, I'll tell you, and if it sucks, I'll tell you that too. And it's funny, I get better responses if something doesn't go well, if it does. So I think, and I don't remember who said it, but someone said, people are going to stick around because they like you. I think it's important to be just a likeable person, just be real, be authentic. And another big thing is don't sell someone if they're not right for your group or for your program, especially if there's coaching involved.
