Well, and you know, I mean, I haven't been on the entire web stream today, but so you might have talked to this point, but you know, I think the experience of, the experience that you talked about when we did the webinar yesterday, right, it was yesterday, about the experience before you decided, you know, two years ago or however long ago around that time, to make funnels, your focus, you jumped around a lot, like to try and figure out, like, you know, there was no doubt that you had mad chops when it came to marketing and internet marketing and after you, you know, after you left strategic profits, you wanted to like claim some space in the internet and so you spent a bunch of time like exploring a lot of different things to use in your businesses to grow your businesses to see which things made the difference. And you know, whether it was traffic, whether it was social media and all those kinds of things and yet at the end of the day, none of those things really matter if when you get someone to your front door that they don't turn into a customer and then a customer that continues to spend money with you and that, you know, is added to a list where you have a great relationship with and, you know, we were talking about Andre Chaperone and how, you know, his list can be smaller than most and yet he can outperform most if not everyone because of the relationship with that list. And so it really comes down to, and you know, and God knows how many conversations we've had about Agora over the years, but you know, Agora's, you can't classify a company as being more basic than them and it really comes down to the basics and part of the basics, you know, the basics evolve over time, but they're nonetheless, they're not the latest and greatest techniques. Funnels are part of the basics. They are what happens when someone first gets introduced to your business and how do they go from prospect to customer to client, to a loyal customer, loyal client and a valuable one. And so really whether it's great copy like Agora really focuses on or whether it's building really great relationships when someone comes through the door like Andre Chaperone does or whether it's the funnel that does both, right, cultivates the relationship and also cultivates the conversion, that's where the people who last and grow and are here today and bigger tomorrow, that's what they focus on because that's all you need to focus on because the better you are at that, the less important everything else is. And you know, you can always find someone to buy traffic for you. You can always find lots of sources of traffic to buy yourself. What you can't find are people who are willing to take your traffic and give you enough money for that traffic on an ongoing basis so that you can just play in the traffic game unless you happen to be a magician when it comes to creating traffic. Right. Right. And so let's talk about that. That's awesome. Let's talk about that whole idea that you talked about that you presented in one of the Founders Club reports just that all traffic has a value, right, and you've got to be able to extract the value. And I've shared with everybody and I really focused on this, I think, in the first video in the pre-launch series on the conversation that you and I had, the advice that you gave me when we were sitting in the Starbucks in Barnes & Noble when you said to me, like, look, Todd, until you can afford to pay for new customers, you don't have a business. You have a promotion. And so can you talk a little bit about that idea, the idea of all traffic has value and you have to be able to extract the value out with your marketing funnel, with your conversion process, just so that everybody really gets it? Sure. And also, like, if anyone who's watching the stream has questions for you and I, I'd be glad to answer anyone's specific questions as well as yours, Todd. I used to have a store and I had a store in Manhattan on Broadway and it's one of the most competitive streets in Manhattan and therefore one of the most competitive retail streets in the world. And nowadays, God knows how much it costs per square foot to rent a store on Broadway, but back in the days when I was renting, it was over $100 a square foot. And the reason why it was $100 a square foot is because of the amount of traffic that was walking past that store every day. So you were guaranteed a certain number of people walking into your store and people who were looking to shop and that's why people are willing to spend, you know, store owners are willing to spend big money per square foot to rent those stores. You know, if I wanted to move the store to some side street that no one walked down, I'd be paying a lot less. If I wanted to be on the third floor of some walk up, you know, on some street that nobody walked by, I could get it really cheap. And the reason why is that I was paying for traffic at the end of the day. I mean, that's why the rent was so high. And it's the same online that, you know, the idea that you're going to find any source of traffic that's free is really, it is a fallacy, right? And it's the way I like to equate it is that, OK, traffic has a value. And so if you can get traffic for free, you can make money, right? Like, you know, if I were to get extra amount of traffic in my business, you know, I'd be able to make this. Well, gold has a value. And you don't think, though, if I could get X amount of gold for free, then I would be rich. But yet we think if we can get X amount of traffic for free, we can be rich. And it really doesn't, that's about the same level of thinking. So all traffic has value because that traffic can be monetized by someone somewhere. And the internet is very efficient at, as a marketplace of the person who's willing to spend money to get the thing and the highest price, the person who's willing to spend the most money, you know, the gentleman that you had on earlier talking about his two rules with Google, Google is going to defer to whoever makes them the most amount of money on the page. Same thing. I mean, that's the whole concept here. They, their real estate has value and they're going to maximize it. All traffic has a value and it's going to go to the source that's willing to spend the most for it and whoever is willing to spend the most for it is going to set the value, right? You know, it's like nothing is worth anything unless someone's willing to buy it, right? So, so all traffic has value because there's always someone who's willing to spend money for it. Now, a lot of people try and get free traffic and they think of the concept of free traffic, but really what they're doing is they're working to get that traffic. So they have to impute some cost to their time and energy and thought and all that. And so they're not really getting it for free. They're getting it for work. So they're trading their effort for that value, right? Now, the, as long as you're in a marketplace where you're, what you're able to extract from an individual visitor, traffic, is more than what the marketplace believes that that traffic is worth. You're really never going to have a traffic problem. And whenever it's less than what the marketplace seems it's worth, you're consistently going to have a traffic problem and you're going to consistently have to do things to get traffic like a bunch of social media stuff or a bunch of this or a bunch of that, because you're trying to make up for the deficiency between what your business can make from traffic and what the traffic is worth. So the founders report was all about helping people understand by going to simple places. These aren't like secrets, like going to the Google keyword tool and getting a sense of what are people willing to spend for traffic in your market for the keywords that you're going to have to where your ideal customers are. What does the marketplace say it's worth? Because if you can make more per visitor than that, then you're already in a place where your business should be getting ready to scale. Now, the odds of you being able to extract what the marketplace deems it's worth from a straight sales letter with no funnel, no nothing is slim to none these days. It wasn't always that way. I mean, you know, when I got online first in like 2002, you know, I had one of the first sites I created was my daughter was a model and was a baby model site. And, you know, I could buy traffic for 25 cents a click on AdWords. And, you know, they went straight to a squeeze page, then to a sales letter. And I think I made like a dollar per visitor. So it was like, I paid a quarter, I made a dollar, and it was like, yeah, it was easy. That doesn't happen anymore. There's a lot more people selling the same stuff and a lot more competition. And so now you need the funnel in place so that you can extract the amount of money that you need so that you don't have a traffic problem. So anytime when someone comes to me and people ask me all the time, like, how do I get more traffic? What they're really saying is, is that my business currently does not get the same value from someone who visits my website as other websites in my market are able to. Can you tell me how to get more traffic? And the answer is no, I cannot tell you how to get more traffic. What I can do is tell you how to fix the problem so that traffic is not the problem. But I can't fix the traffic problem because there really isn't a traffic problem. What you're telling me is, is that you're like, you know, rich every time someone gives me a dollar bill, I'm able to turn it into 90 cents. Right. Can you tell me how to get more dollar bills? No, I can't. I can tell you how to get every dollar bill that's given to you to be worth a dollar 10 and then you won't have any difficulty finding dollar bills. See, that is so insanely powerful. And that's why I have you on here to explain things in ways that I could never. And for everybody, what you just heard was if you were really paying attention was that first lesson that I learned that I told you about earlier, right? What Rich is saying is like, look, in your market, traffic, a visitor is worth a certain amount of money. Right. And if your marketing funnel, if your marketing, if your conversion vehicle cannot extract out at least that much or more, you are going to have a problem generating traffic, but it's not really a traffic generation problem. The problem is that it's the conversion vehicle. It's the ability to extract out from the traffic what the traffic is worth and more. See, that's what I want you to understand. It's like I said before, you know, that I came through this realization with Rich's advice that there really are no traffic problems, especially today. There are more channels, more sources, more opportunities than there's ever been before to generate traffic. You know, earlier with Ben, we were talking about retargeting, remarketing, right? That wasn't available, you know, when I first got online. There is, you know, LinkedIn advertising, Twitter advertising, Facebook advertising, these things that weren't available years ago when I first got online. So it's not from a lack of sources that most marketers are struggling to grow their business, sources of traffic. The reality is that they're not getting enough traffic because they can't afford to generate the traffic because every time they do, they lose money because the traffic is worth more than what they can extract out of it. See, and that's why you need a marketing funnel. The marketing funnel is what extracts out that money for you. It's the marketing funnel that takes the dollar, right? And it brings you back the dollar and more, right? So you can afford to continue to take that dollar and turn it into more visitors, more money. Take that dollar, continue to do it over and over and over and over again. And so what Rich explained to you is just, I hope that you really got it. And so, you know, so keep going. So sorry about that. Okay, yeah, no worries. And I'm like, I'm drawing a blank here. I'm forgetting the number now. I don't remember if it was like, you know, the overall campaign, like everybody's heard, right? It was made like a quarter of a billion dollars. But the, but I don't remember now if it was 30 million or 6 million that they made off of TV advertiser profit, right? Front end profit. And the, so they had a sales letter. You know, and Angora is the exception to the rule, right? They write 500 sales letters a year. They're hoping to find the one a decade that can do this, right? Because they're trying to do it in one sales letter in that particular case, not even in a funnel, but, you know, the way that I would do it, the way you would do it in the way that anyone would be watching, because we're not going to have the world's greatest copywriters writing for us for like, you know, over a hundred copywriters writing for a decade to try to do that. There's a man whose predictions have come true, blah, blah, blah, blah. And then it was a link to the video sales letter. And like I said, I don't remember if they made six million dollars profit, just front end profit selling a, you know, $47 newsletter or 30 million advertising and on cable. But what did they do? Like they had something, right? This sales letter that they first tested to their lists and it worked. They then rolled it out to Facebook and like online and every and through banners and it worked. And then they wanted to get more traffic because the thing worked and they knew that if they get people to watch it to the page for less than why they were going to be making X and so what they did was they went to radio, they went to cable. What they did not do is buy a course on cable advertising, learning how to get cable advertising at a discount, learning the secret tricks of cable advertising or finding gurus of cable advertising or anything like that. They had a conversion process that worked and they were able to go to cable and it worked because they were able to buy the visitors at a price that worked. Right? Now what most people do online is not that. They don't build the funnel that works. They try to find the guru of social media that's going to help them get their traffic for cheap or the guru of banner advertising or pay-per-view advertising or whatever. And I'm not knocking those guys because they do serve a purpose. If you've got a converting thing, sure, you want to get the most amount of traffic for the least cost and these guys can be very helpful to you, but only once you have something to push people through that actually turns people into money, they're not going to make up the difference between your bad conversion process and a good conversion process. You know, and all those guys would be the first to say they're a dream client is someone who's got something that's already working really well that they can then just really rip open the doors and send lots of traffic to and not have to babysit and try and get the person to get the conversion rates up and everything else. So, Rich, one of the greatest lessons that that I really learned from you and I learned it very early on was this whole idea of being strategic versus being strategic rather than being tactical. And so can you just talk a little bit about what it means to be strategic as it relates to your marketing funnel and developing and setting up your marketing funnel versus just being tactical as it relates to setting it up? Sure. You know, the I could, but I can go a lot of different routes with it. So anything in particular or? No, I just, you know, you know, I've shared before with clients this whole idea that like, look, you know, you know, every tactic serves a strategic purpose or every tactic that you use should should serve a strategic purpose. And so it's this question of why, why, why am I doing this? What is it that I'm trying to have happen from this this tactic? And so I just know that I really learned really I became strategic thanks to you. And so just in that arena, like when you're deciding practically what to do. Well, even before you decide tactically what you should do, you should be clear on what the ultimate outcome is. And for the overwhelming majority, I would say of entrepreneurs period, let alone online offline doesn't matter. They're not. They they think maybe one or two steps ahead and they think they're being strategic. But they're not really thinking long term end game like what's what's the ultimate outcome I want to happen. So, you know, just to go back to the retail store for a second, because it's a really easy example to kind of talk about and then we can apply it to online is that, you know, the average person, the average store, when someone walked into their store, their goal was to sell that person. Some clothes, if it's a clothing store, right? And but our goal was to sell the person on the store first, because if we could sell them on the store, the likelihood of them coming back was much higher and to come back consistently. So if we could sell them that the store was the greatest place in the world to be, then we'll still have the chance to sell them the clothes. But we'll also have a much higher likelihood of the person really being sold on the store because it wasn't going to happen by accident. So what we did for that was that instead of salespeople asking customers when they came in, like, can I help you get anything? Which always the answer is no.
